[Column 1]
THE SABLE MESSENGER.

There was grief in the heart of the mother—
	A sorrow unspeakably deep,
And the loving young sister and brother
	Stole out of the chamber to weep.
Each friend that stood by felt the shadow,
	The cold, creeping shadow of woe,
As in trembling and tears they awaited
	The moment when Leah must go.

Afar to some beautiful country
	Beyond the wide horizon’s rim,
(So far and so lovely none ever
	Returned from its boundaries dim;)
Her father had years ago journeyed,
	Left wife and sweet little ones all—
But here is a messenger from him,
	His dearly loved Leah to cal[-].

“Thy father has sent for thee, Leah,”
	The messenger whispering said,
And stood with his long sable mantle
	Enveloping even his head.
’Twas rumored that never a mortal
	Dared look in the face such as he,
Lest eyesight be blasted or even
	He cease of the living to be,

How black was that long shrouding m[-]ntle!
	How hollow that voice and how low!
And Leah clung close to her mother,
	And shuddered, and dreaded to go.
“O mother, I fear this dark stranger,”
	She panted in tremulous tone,
“I know not the way—is there danger?
	Oh, must I go, mother, alone?”

Close, close to the heart of the mother
	Her tender arms folded her child,
And, torn with unspeakble sorrow,
	She pleaded in agony wild—
“Oh, spare me this trial, I pray thee! 
Oh, break not a mother’s fond heart!
Return by the way which thou camest;
	And alone, oh in mercy! depart.”
 
There were tears in the eyes of the mother,
	And sobbing outside on the stair,
And sympathy saddened each o[-]her,
	Who waited expectantly there.
But sternly unmoved stood the stranger, 
As still as a statue of Fate;
He stirred not a fold of his mantle
	But icily whispered “I wait ”

The moon hid her face in a cloud-vail
	The tremulous stars ceased to shine,
The wind with its eerie low moaning
	Swept mournfully through the dark pine.
All sound faltered low into si[-]ence—
	Fond nature seemed holding her breath—
The thought sped from bosom to bosom,
	“How dreadful how cruel is death!”

[Column 2]
At length with a slow, stately motion,
	The stranger a step drew more near,
And Leah her startled eyes lifted,
	All darkened and heavy with fear.
He opened his arms to enfo[-]d her.
	The pall drew aside from his brow,
And lo! the dear face of her father
	Smiled lovingly over her now.

What glorious flash no[-] illumines
	The sweet face so pallid and worn!
What luster those heavy eyes brightens,
	Light sunshine above a dark storm!
“Papa!” cried the sweet voice, and lifting
	Frail hands like the wings of a bird,
“Goodbye, dear mamma,” low she whispered,
	And sprang to his arms at the word

There were sighs from the lone hearted mother,
	Caressing the pale, lovely clay;
There were tears from the sister and brother,
	For Leah had vanished away;
But the smile of her joy softly lingered
	Around the cold, beautiful face,
That seemed to her dear ones remaining
	A token of heavenly grace.
						[-] U DALTON


INTERNATIONAL COUNCIL OF
			WOMEN. 

	A conference of the International Coun-
cil of Women convened in London July 5
and 6, to make preparations for the quin-
quennial session of the Council which will
be held in London in the summer of 1899.
	The conference was open only to mem-
bers of the Executive Committee and the
Committee on Arrangements. The first day
was occupied by a business session of the
Executive Committee; the second was de-
voted by the Committee of Arrangements
to discussing the proposition relating to the
Quinquennial already submitted by the dif-
ferent National Councils composing the In-
ternational.
	The United States was represented by 
Mrs. May Wright Sewall, who went over
to preside over the conference in the en-
forced absence of Lady Aberdeen. Mrs.
Alice Wheeler Pierce and Miss Octavia
Williams Bates who went as delegates, and
the Countess di Brazza, who sat in the con-
ference as the proxy of the president of the
National Council of Women of the United
States.—Ex.


CLARA BARTON’S REPORT. 

	New York, July 16.—A telegram re-
ceived today by the executive committee of
the Red Cross from Miss Clara Barton dated
Playa Del Este, July 15, is as follows:
	Santiago has surrendered. I came from
the front yesterday in a pouring rain.
Fever is suspected here. Siboney is burned.
Dr. Lesser and Mrs. Lesser and all their
nurses are in the fever hospital two miles
distant. I saw them today and all are doing
well. Four army surgeons of the St. Paul
are with us, there being no other provision
for them.
	We are feeding the refugees of Sibony,
and many thousands at Eurmisea, by rail.


Elwell is feeding them at Caney by army
wagons, and 20,000 by pack mules, 15
miles travel.
	Commander McCalla of the Marblehead,
called yesterday for 100,000 rations, medi-
cines and clothing for the refugees in the
woods in the country surrounding Guan-
tanamo. Nothing is known of boats or of
aid of any kind from any other source.
State of Texas has fed all the wounded at
the front, and is helping them home.
	All members of the Red Cross are in per-
fect health, thoroughly organized.
	A telegram has been received from Red 
Cross Agent Kent at Jacksonville who
states that Col. Maus, surgeon-in-chief,
has just returned from Miami and reports
much sickness there, with a great need of
milk and ice. The Red Cross launch
Moyner has been repaired and will be
ready to leave Jacksonville Monday, for
Key West.
	Advices just received from Key West in-
dicate that the Red Cross will reach Santia- 
go Tuesday next with the much needed
supplies and surgeons.


MRS. GATES’ LETTER.

Provo, July 19, 1898.
My Dear Aunt Em:

	After the convention closed in Denver on
June 27th I remained in Denver, as you
know, as the guest of Apostle John W.
Taylor. I had a most delightful visit with
Brother Taylor and his kind, gentle wife,
receiving many attentions also from the
Elders in that mission; I must mention the
kindness of Elders Ensign and Graham,also
the unfailing courtesy of Elder White, not
forgetting the thoughtfulness of Brother
Pack and the exceeding sweetness of Elder
Clive’s magic violin. In short all the
Elders were as good to me as if I had been
their own mother, and I shall not soon for-
get my visit nor the pleasant friendships re-
newed and formed.
	Apostle Taylor called the sisters of the
Relief Society together in Denver and we
spent an hour or so at Sister Taylor’s talk-
ing upon the duties and privileges of this
organization. Sister Lowery, a lady from
Boston, is the president of the organization
there. She is of a very mild and yet strong-
character with perfect manners and a will- 
ing, helpful disposition. She is very anx-
ious for more light and assistance in her
duties, and I recommended her to take
the "Exponent" and communicate with
you. There are thirty sisters in this branch,
who are very earnest and faithful members.
	The next branch visited was Colorado
Springs which has just been organized since
last February. There are only four fami-
lies in this branch and all the women be- 
long to the Relief Society. Sister Lenora
Adams is the president with Sister Dena
Myers as first and Alice Johnson as second 
counselors and Susie Johnson as secretary
and treasurer. I gave them some iustruc-
tion as to the duties of officers, mode of con-
ducting meetings and blessed them as the


Spirit suggested in their labors.  It was
very interesting to examine thus the be-
ginning of the great work which grows al-
ways with uplifting power. All these
branches and the branches of the Church
are of very recent origin and in great con-
trast to the old, well established organiza-
tions which I have attended in my life.
	At Pueblo Apostle Taylor joined us and
we held three public meetings. Also or-
ganized the Relief Society.
	We have great reason to rejoice in the
great blessings meted out to the Saints and
for the rapid spread and increase of the
work of God. I trust you yourself will
some day be able to visit these faithful and
earnest sisters of the Colorado mission.
			Yours lovingly,
				SUSA YOUNG GATES.


WOMAN S EXPONENT


THE MOTHER’S CONGRESS.

Dear Sister Wells:
	Now that the Congress festivities of the
“Mothers of America” are over, both in
national capacity and state as far as our own
dear Utah is concerned—and I have been
privileged to be an active participant in the
one and a silent observer in the other—I
feel impressed to write a few words of in-
formation for those who may be interested
in the move to advance motherhood.
	As far as our Utah Congress of Mothers
is concerned, I was a silent observer and of
course am not informed enough to express
my views; but at the National Congress of
Mothers I had the honor of being an active
participant, and as such can speak facts.
The only conclusion I can come to (according
to my silent observations) is that too few
of our Utah people are acquainted with the
effort that has been made here in regard to
educating women for motherhood. In gen-
eral terms this manner of education has
been in operation here in Utah from the be-
ginning of its existence, through the Relief
Society and later the Young Ladies Asso-
ciation, but later still a special work for
the education of motherhood has been in
operation, and of which I give myself the
credit of being the originator. It is now
between nine and ten years since I started
this special work here in Utah for the sole
purpose of better qualifying women for
motherhood, and one of the consequences
was that I was invited to attend the Nation-
al Congress of Mothers, at Washington, D.
C., this spring as delegate to represent my
work. I went there to represent about fif-
teen hundred women of Utah and bring
their greeting to the Congress. 
	About a year ago blanks were sent to me
from headquarters at Washington, which
would unquestionably give an understand-
ing of the nature of my work.
	In brief, I will say that I went to that
blessed Congress at Washington, and the
privileges I enjoyed, the honor and respect
conferred upon me was something that I
never dreamed would ever be my lot. I
went with comfort and hope in my soul,
having been set apart for the mission and
the blessings promised were literally ful-
filled. The first day of Congress I gave
my report like the other delegates. It was
listened to with great interest which was
proven from the enthusiasm manifested
through applause. It was the only work
of its nature reported and seemed to fill a 
vacant place. I gave my report verbally,
which was very much appreciated, so much

[Column 2]
so that the president took me warmly by
the hand saying: “Oh I am so glad, that
was from the heart; that is the way I wish
you to speak tomorrow.” I was on the
program for the next day, to treat the sub-
ject, “The Physical Side of Motherhood.”
	I went to the meeting with my soul full
of gratitude that I would be privileged to
present to this important assemblage of wo-
men the principles that are so dear to me,
and which I think so important to the true
adornment of the human race. I went
there fasting and praying, and my Heaven-
ly Father blessed me, I know. I had no
paper, I spoke of my subject as I knew it.
I know it was appreciated by the audience
from the enthusiasm manifested. After the
applause ceased several written notes were
sent up to me (by the ushers) of congratula-
tion and appreciation, asking for interviews
with me at the close of the meeting. One
lady from New York wrote me: “I am go-
ing right home and organize such work. I
would give anything if I could get you
along with me to start us out with your
counsel and advice. Please let me have an
interview with you after this meeting.”
When I came down from the platform and
the meeting was closed it seemed that every
one wanted to shake my hand and be intro-
duced to “the delegate from Utah,” and to
tell me who they were. The next morning
ing when I came and took my seat a beau-
tiful bouquet of flowers was handed over to
me with the words: “This is in apprecia-
tion of what you said yesterday. I am
delegate from Arkansas.”
	I would not have you think that I am
speaking of these things in a boasting spirit,
oh no, far from it! but it is in honor of the
noble women, and with the fulness of my
soul’s gratitude to my Heavenly Father. I
have no honor for myself, it is all His.
	It was so intensely desired that I should
have held a special conference, they called
it, in my line of work, and it was tried
hard every day during the week to get that
brought about by all those who were so en-
thusiastic—the president as much as any
one—but the program was so extensive and
time so very crowded, that it was not pos-
sible to get the time for it, and when I said
goodbye the beloved president said warmly
to me: “I am so sorry we could not get to
hold that conference, but you must be with
us again, we must hear more from you.”
And she added, “I am so glad we have just
received a telegram today from Salt Lake
City stating that they are organizing a
Mothers’ Congress, so you will be right
in it when you get home.” I thanked her
very heartily for her kind feeling and the
way she had treated me, and was silent for
the balance.
	When it was found that it was not pos-
sible to hold that special conference, I obli-
gated myself by promise to many delegates
to send them after I returned all the infor-
mation I could in writing concerning my
work. I have now done so, in as much as
have sent to eleven states in America
long, long letters of explanations, accom-
panied by my little book “What Women
Should Know.” I have done it on the
missionary plan, thankful if I can in this
way be instrumental in the hands of God to
bring some light to those who wish to have
it.
	I say in honor of that blessed woman, the
president of the National Congress, Mrs.
Theodore W. Birney, that she is a model
of grace, humility and symplicity. One is

[Column 3]
drawn towards her with the love of a sister.
God bless her in her noble calling!
	I have a word more to say in honor of
our esteemed Senator at Washington,
Hon. Frank J. Cannon and wife, for their
great kindness and consideration towards
me while at Washington. I shall never for-
get it and my soul is full of blessings for
them and all theirs, that God will reward
them for their manifestations of love and
respect to their humble sister.
			HANNAH SORENSEN.
P. S. I forgot to say that I have had a
most beautiful letter from the president of 
the National Congress of Mothers since I
came home—a letter of sympathy and en-
couragement asking also for information in
my line of work, and it finishes with the
words: “You must be with us again.”

	While on my sojourn in the East I had
also the privilege of visiting Chicago for a
period of five weeks. It was a very plea-
sant experience I had there in many ways,
but what I especially wish to refer to there
is the organization, or branch of our Church
organization there. I did not expect to find
such a flourishing little branch there as I
did, so I received very unexpectedly a very
pleasant impression. Our Elders laboring
there are types of intelligent men full of
ambition and energy, but the most beauti-
ful ornamentation I found in these dear
brethren was the unmistakable stamp of
humility. It was so gratifying to me to
silently notice the interest, love, grace and
respect which these dear brethren mani-
fested toward their little flock when they
met to worship the Lord. I should per-
haps not say little flock, for I think the
average attendance on Sundays was fifty or
sixty, and I think that is very good when
we consider that it is a new mission, so to
speak. They have a beautiful hall where
they hold their meetings which would do
honor to any society be they ever so aristo-
cratic, but the best of it was that they had
the most perfect order and cleanliness mani-
fest on all occasions.	*	*	*
	I attended several of the meetings of the
little branch of the Relief Society while
there, held in private houses among the
sisters who belong to the Society. It num-
bers about fourteen members, I believe,
who as a rule are all present except in case
of sickness. Their president, a very esti-
mable woman, is a Sister Smith who a few
years back was a resident of Nephi here in
Utah, but on account of circumstances re-
sides at present in Chicago. She is well
qualified for the position she occupies, in-
telligent, modest and warm-hearted, and
has the respect of those who preside in the
branch. Their secretary, Sister Ericksen,
is a very efficient secretary, punctual and
orderly, and in short it was a group of sis-
ters in whose company I enjoyed myself
very much. Their methods of meeting
were of the same order that we have here
at home with two exceptions (and these ex-
ceptions were very pleasant features) name-
ly, the members were regular attendants
and of the Elders of the mission two or
three were also regular attendants.
							H. S.


The Adams Express company has an-
nounced that it will carry packages of goods
from relatives to the soldiers in any of the
camps in the South, or to the naval for-
warding points, at 25 per cent below the
usual express rates.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


ANGELS' WHISPERS

Sweeter than the breath of roses
	Lingering on the dewy air,
Wafting as the daylight closes
	To my heart bowed down with care,
Blessed thought that Father loves me,
		And doth hear my fervent prayer.

Gentle words in softest accent,
	Whispered in my listening ear:
Weep not for thy boy though absent,
	Dry that secret burning tear;
Thy Creator knows and loves thee!
	Lift thy soul and do not fear.

Guarding e'er with unseen power
	Sheltering him so far away,
Though where shot and shell doth shower,
	He the missiles flight can stay,
Ever trust the God of Battles!
	Live thou near Him day by day.
					ELLIS R. SHIPP. 
July 23, 1898.


[Column 1]
HOTEL METROPOLE.

Avalon, Santa Catalina Island, Cal.
					July 8, 1898.
Dr. Pratt, President, and ladies of the
		Press Club:
	I send you my most cordial and affection-
ate greetings; my sincere wishes for a
pleasant and restful summer.
	I will tell you as briefly as I can in a
letter what I have been doing since I left
Utah. Two weeks were given to San Fran-
cisco and vicinity, with frequent calls at
Camp Merritt. The boys were quite well
and a credit to their state, but disappointed
that they were left as home guards when
they expected to sail soon for the Philip-
pines; but they need not be impatient they
may have their chance for active service
and destruction. While it looks now as if
the end were not far in the future, I am one
who believes the war will be long, I have
never felt quite sure it would end in a few
weeks. My sojourn was pleasant as I have
many dear friends there, while much time
was devoted to sight seeing.
	The last of June we came to Los Angeles,
“City of the Angels,” the garden of Eden
as one friend remarked, and truly with
Pasadena, Riverside and Santa Monica it is
the garden spot of California, though not so
beautiful as when I visited the place four
years ago—owing to the drouth. As usual
we had an earthquake, light said the people,
but it wakened me from a sound sleep and
my bed rocked three times and it was noisy.
A friend at Redoude said there was a wall
of kelp several feet high in front of their
cottage, so there must have been quite a
disturbance somewhere. We visited the
ostrich farm, not nearly so interesting as
the one at Phoenix, Arizona, the birds are
not so large.
	One of the most lovely halting places of
my journey was at Avalon, on Santa Cata-
lina Island. The train took us to San
Pedro harbor, we embarked on the steamer
Falcon and were soon plowing the blue
waters of Santa Catalina channel, the island
is about twenty miles distant. The trip
across is a delightful experience, we were
told, that a person must be extremely sen-
sitive to suffer with sea sickness, but a large
majority were sensitive. The sky is clear,
the water blue, the air balmy, the flying
fishes dart from the waves and fly an eighth
of a mile. Something over two hours sail
and the magic island is reached. Santa

[Column 2]
Catalina is an island twenty-two miles long;
greatest width seven and a half miles; cir-
cumference sixty miles; area fifty-five
thousand acres. It abounds in lofty moun-
tains, deep gorges, stupendous rocks, cliffs
and precipices, and in winter is a semi-
tropic garden.
	Avalon, upon the bay of that name, is
the fashionable resort of Southern Califor-
nia. The Metropole is the principal hotel,
there are cottages and a city of tents, which
many prefer to the comforts and conven-
ences of the hotels. From C. F. Holder’s
“South Catalina and Isles of Summer.”
“The discovery within the present decade
of some papers in a public library of Mad-
rid, Spain, which proved to be an account
of the voyage of Juan Rodriguez Cabrillo,
a Portuguese navigator in the service of
Spain.
	“The caravels of Cabrillo La Victoria and
San Salvador, discovered the islands in
September, 1542; and as they cruised in
the vicinity for several months there is
every reason to believe that the quaint ves-
sels found shelter in the harbor of Avalon
and the land-locked bay of Catalina on the
south coast, and that the explorers held
communication with the natives who at
that time had towns or villages in the
mouth of almost every cañon of the island.
	Cabrillo named the islands of San
Clemente after his ships, and probably
landed at Avalon where it is known was a
large and populous Indian village on the
site of Hotel Metropole.
	“Sixty years later Philip III. of Spain
ordered the viceroy of New Spain, Gaspar
Yuñiga, Conde de Monterey, to equip an
expedition, which sailed from Acapullo
under the command of Sebastian Vizcaino,
May 5, 1602. About the first of December
the caravels came to anchor in the harbor
of Avalon and Vizcaino called the island
Santa Catalina. He describes the natives
as a fine looking race dressed in well cured
skins, the town as having large dwellings
and numerous rancherios, their canoes were
large, the men were expert fishermen.
They showed Vizcaino some damask cloth
which they said came from a wreck, sup-
posed to be that of the ship San Augustin
which sailed in 1595 under Captain Sebas-
tian Rodriguez Cermeñon, to make dis-
coveries on the California coast and was
never heard from.
	“When Cabrillo discovered Southern
California it had a large population; today
352 years later this is represented by a
pitiful handful of natives who are scat-
tered about the foothills or the missions;
by graveyards despoiled and by the quaint
stone implements plowed up by the modern
ranchers.”
	We went out in the glass-bottomed boat,
the waters are so clear that objects at a
depth of fifty or more feet can be seen dis-
tinctly. You gaze down into the depths
and most wonderful are the sights. Among
the moving beds of kelp with long green
and bronze fronds reaching upward, float-
ing with the tide were the gold fish, the
man would call our attention to the differ-
ent fish and tell us where to look. See that
delicate green-hued kelp fish, then a black
ray, there an eel, sea serpent, an octopus,
sea snails, starfishes, sea urchins and a tiny
blue fish, and in the shallow waters the
rocks were covered with sea-anemone, and
there were most beautiful shells—altogether
a beautiful and fairy like spectacle.
	We had the drive, or island coach ride in
which the six in hand takes the rider up

[Column 3]
the mountains and over the summits to Lit-
tle Harbor. The coach road winds up the
hill to the south to a height of 1600 feet,
every turn brings new bits of coast into
view, with headlands, mountains and hills
and the blue waters until the summit is
reached, where the view is most beautiful
of San Clemente, Santa Barbara Island, San
Nicolas, the lofty range of the Sierra Madre,
all forming a beautiful panorama. I
bought a curiosity, the Serebratula, ob-
tained by deep sea soundings, and is the
shell the Romans took their idea of the Ro-
man lamp from. It is rare and very old.
	I began this letter then laid it aside.
Have written it at intervals and a portion
blew away. I will send what is left of it
with best wishes.
			HELEN V. KIMBALL.


AN EMINENT VISITOR.
	Miss Sarah E. Hunt of Boston, first vice-
president of the Daughters of the Revolu-
tion general society, and regent of the
Massachusetts State society, visited our city
during the Mothers’ Congress. Miss Hunt
is a leading woman and Mrs. Wells was
fortunate in arranging a meeting between
her and the Wasatch chapter of the D. R.
	The “Exponent” office was beautifully
decorated with roses. Miss Hunt talked in
a simple, charming manner, enthusing her
interested listeners. Miss Hunt said:
“The North and the South in the Revolu-
tion went to war together and fought for
one common cause side by side, and we are
their children. Between the North and the
South, the East and the West there is that
bond that cannot be broken, this is the
grandest part of our work. The D. R. has
a large field. Just now there is a chance to
show what we can do in instilling patriot-
ism. The Massachusetts State society is
very successful, we have a large member-
ship. Our best work is in repairing and
preserving the Paul Revere, Bunker Hill
and others, but we feel that our greatest
strength is in our State society.
	“Do not feel disappointed, Mrs. Wells,
if mothers feel that they are so busy that
they cannot attend to joining, keep a brave
heart and never give up even a poor ground.
We desire Mrs. Wells to be State Regent
of your society. Be Democratic so that all
eligible persons may come in. If there are
any daughters of men who fought in the
Revolution find them and admit them free
of fees into the D. R. The General Soci-
ety will present them with a gold badge.
We want to especially honor them.
	“Our Massachsuttes State society found
an old lady aged 104, who was the daughter
of a soldier of the Revolution and we took
great pride in presenting her with a gold
badge.
	If any of you ladies come to New York
be sure you call at our headquarters.
Thursdays we have tea. ” MissHunt has a
charming presence and won the hearts of
the members of the Wasatch chapter.
	After the goodbyes were said Mrs. Wells
asked “How many of the roses will you
take with you?” and the reply came, “As
many as you will give me, and as many as
my hands can hold.” Then with the
pleasant and good wishes for a delightful
trip through the Yellowstone whither she
is going, Miss Hunt carried away a great
bunch of the lovely roses.
A. M. H.

SALT LAKE CITY AUGUST 1, 1898.


MAINE MARTYR MEMORIAL DAY.

To the Presidents of Stakes and the
Bishops of the Church:
	Dear Brethren:—In view of the fact that 
the management of the Maine Monument
Fund has disclaimed any intentional slight
on the part of the national committee to-
wards the Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-
day Saints by omiting on the 8th day of
May, to call upon the Church to join the
other churches in the land in holding
memorial services on the 8th day of May,
in honor of the brave men who lost their
lives in the Maine disaster; and now ask us
through its management to appoint a suit-
able day on which the members of the
Church may hold Memorial services and
take up collections for the Maine Monument
Fund, we have decided to designate Sun-
day, the 24th of July, as the day on which
such services shall be held.
	It is our desire that the Church of Jesus
Christ of Latter-day Saints should be iden-
tified with all such patriotic movements as
the erection of this monument to the
nation’s honored dead, that all our fellow
citizens may know that our sympathies
both in its joys and in its sorrows, are with
our country. We therefore ask the Presi-
dents of Stakes and the Bishops of wards
throughout the Church to make the neces-
sary arrangements for holding special
Memorial services at the time designated,
and at the conclusion thereof to take up a
collection from the people to assist in the
erection of a monument to the Maine mar-
tyrs.
	All money collected should be promptly
forwarded by the Bishops to Elders Heber
J. Grant and B. H. Roberts, who have
been appointed a committee to take charge
of this business. Address them at 20—26
south, East Temple street, Salt Lake City,
Utah. Your Brethren,
WILFORD WOODRUFF,
GEORGE Q. CANNON.
JOSEPH F. SMITH.


Mrs. L. M. Stansbury of Colorado who is
pleasantly remembered in Utah and known
to Western readers by her pen name, Ellis
Meredith, has won the fifth prize in the
$4,000 prize story competition conducted
by the Short Story Publishing Company,
publishers of the Black Cat magazine.
Thousands of manuscripts were received
from every quarter of the globe.


SPECIAL NOTICE N. W. R. S.

	The Summer Conference of the Nation-
al Woman’s Relief Society which was
postponed from June until August, will
not be held this season on account of ex-
isting circumstances that were not antici-
pated at the time the appointment was
made at the April conference last spring.
The General conference of the Society
will be held as usual in October, of which
due notice will be given.

ZINA D. H. YOUNG, General President.
E. B. WELLS, General Secretary.


SAD AND SORROWFUL.

Ah, the sweet young rose of hope is dead—
	'Twill never bloom again
And the tears I shed for the beautiful dead,
	They fall like the desolate rain.

	The sorrow that has fallen so heavily up-
on Brother and Sister Gates of Provo through
the terrible accident on the evening of
July 21, fills all hearts with grief, who know
the family. One stands overwhelmed in
such an hour, and it is only by seeking the
Lord one can find consolation at all, He
has promised and His word cannot fail of
fulfillment:

	“When thro’ fiery trials thy pathway shall lie,
	My grace all sufficient shall  e thy supply.
	The flames shall not hurt thee I only design
	Thy dross to consume and thy gold to refine.”

	Little Beulah Gates, seven years of age,
was a child of promise and of faith in
God, exemplified in the last hour by her
kneeling to pray after (though she knew
it not) she was past all human aid. What
a tribute to her mother was this child’s
dying prayer! and may the beautiful les-
son be impressed upon the parents far and
wide; “teach the children to pray.”
	The funeral services were excellent, we
are told, and great consolation was offered
to the bereaved parents. We know
“God moves in a mysterious way,” and
we must acknowledge His hand in all
things however hard the lesson may be

	And if the ordeal seems cruel
		The father will make it right
	And the brighter thy precious jewel
		Will shine in t e realms of light.


[Column 1]
WOMAN’S WORK IN WAR.

	Nowadays one has daily the war and the
news pertaining thereto with all its varia-
tions of battles fought and unfought on sea
and on land. To publish a paper that con-
tained no war news at all would be an ex-
ception to the general rule, for we have
war news the first thing in the morning
and the last thing at night, and one dreams
of the war waking or sleeping; and yet to
give anything like a summary of reliable
war dispatches in a little paper only pub-
lished at intervals of two weeks or more is
out of the question.
	The news which makes one fear and
tremble at present is the sickness prevailing
in those hot climates where our soldiers are,
and if the people ever need to exercise
mighty faith it is in view of the fevers pre-
valent in the camps of the soldiers in hot
weather. When one reads that Gen. Shafter
reports 3,770 cases in the army in Cuba,
which it is said gives the war department

[Column 2]
concern, how is it with the mothers, wives
and sisters as well as sweethearts of the
men and boys who have gone forth in the
full strength and glory of their physical
health to fight for their country? It is in-
deed a serious matter and when women feel
they can only watch and pray at home how
difficult it is to be reconciled. Tennyson
says:

			‘More things are wrought by prayer,
Than this world dreams of. Wherefore let thy
	voice
Rise like a fountain for them night and day.”

Joanna Bailey:
					“A goodman’s prayers
Will from the deepest dungeon climb heaven’s
	height
And bring a blessing down”

Young in his “Night Thoughts:”

“Prayer ardent opens heaven, lets down a stream
Of glory on the consecrated hour
Of man in audience with the Deity,”

	Coleridge in the Ancient Mariner: “He
prayeth best who loveth best.” And wo-
man it is conceded, loves most and truest
her dear ones therefore should never cease
to pray, God grant us grace to do our part
as women if it be only in prayer, and let us
also pray for peace.
	But there is work to be done for the sick
and distressed and all who can should lend
a helping hand to the Red Cross and War
Relief Association in some of the various
methods and ways in which actual help is
being given to those who have not the op-
portunity of doing or getting necessary
things for their own comfort and relief or to
prevent pain or suffering.
	It is difficult to understand how women
can sit idly by and not endeavor to aid in
some way those who at this particular time
need positive help, and so as well as to
watch and pray, there is also work; and to
be at work is sometimes a panacea for pain
of mind or heart. Those who want to
give and have not the means, some of them
at least can give time and thought and by
the needle and in various other ways assist
in the Red Cross or Relief work. A few
women are giving of their time, their
means, their hearts to this noble work for
those deprived of home and friends and
the things they are now needing and to
which they have been accustomed when in
the home circle. The widow’s mite is just
as acceptable now as when our Savior was
upon the earth, and the beautiful saying
holds good everywhere and at all times,
and so one should not hesitate to give a lit-
tle.
	The overtures from Spanish government-
al powers are somewhat encouraging in
favor of an armistice but not altogether ac-
ceptable, the taking of Manila, the victory
of Santiago, Dewey’s wonderful achieve-
ments, and all the advantages so far known
do not give us peace and there is great un-
certainty as to the future, and the sorrows
and cruelties and barbarisms of war make
one shudder at the very thought, and to be
in the midst of such scenes is more than
terrible.
	We had hoped to give here an account of
Red Cross work in the State but could not
get an entire report nor an approximate, we
know, however, that it has been very credit-
able and will give the figures and particu-
lars later.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


PIONEER PARK.

	On Monday, July 25, fifty-one years after
the arrival of the Pioneers in the valley of
the Great Salt Lake, the square on which
the first human habitations were built in
this desert land (now a flourishing state)
was publically dedicated with appropriate
and impressive ceremonies, conducted un-
der the auspices of the Mayor and the City
Council. President Wilford Woodruff, the
veteran pioneer now ninety-one years of
age, was seated upon the platform, also
President George Q. Cannon, who came in-
to the valley a few months later, and a few
members of the old City Council of long
ago and other citizens, also a few promin-
ent women, among the number being Mrs.
Zina D. H. Young, Mrs. Jane B. Taylor,
Mrs. Maria L. Nebeker; the two last named
came in 1847. The patriotic music was
inspiring, the singing excellent, opening
prayer by Dr. T. C. Iliff and address by
Mayor Clark both particularly well adapt-
ed to the occasion, the Mayor rehearsing
the most notable events. Our revered
President made the speech of the day, heart-
felt and replete with memories of the time
alluded to.
		ADDRESS OF PRESIDENT WOODRUFF.

	President Woodruff was then presented to the audience by Mayor Clark and was
received with the warmest demonstration
of applause. The venerable Pioneer and
leader said in substance:
	It was fifty-one years ago yesterday a
little before 11 o’clock a. m., that he drove
President Young in his carriage on to the
spot near where the Knutsford hotel stands.
On his arrival in the valley, the speaker
had, he said, one bushel and a half of pota-
toes in his possession. He had covenanted
with the Lord that he would neither eat
nor drink until they were planted, and he
kept his promise. Others of the Pioneers
also had potatoes with them. They, too,
promptly attended to the work of planting.
And they were planted in the earth, not in
the moon, (dark or light) as Brother Orson Pratt objected to that. (Laughter.)
	There was a man in Nauvoo who built a
carriage to suit his own fancy. It had a
bed eleven feet long and was entered by
means of steps and door. This vehicle the
speaker bought and brought to this valley.
On the Monday morning immediately after
the arrival of the first Pioneers President
Young took some of the Twelve Apostles
and with them walked on a short distance
in a northwesterly direction; a halt was sud-
denly made and President Young striking
his cane upon the ground, exclaimed,
"Brethren, here will be the Temple of our
God.” "I didn’t ask him,” continued
President Woodruff, “who told him the
Temple would be there. I didn’t care. I
put a stake there and the Temple is there,
a monument to President Young’s foresight
and prophetic accuracy.”
	After passing over the ground which is
now Pioneer square the party entered
wagons and started for the point of the
mountain, which appeared to be about five
miles to the westward. The distance, how- 
ever, was much greater, being about fifteen
miles. They continued on to Black Rock 
where President Young plunged in the brine
and took a bath, being the first Mormon to
lave in its waters. He was followed by
other members of the party among whom
was a colored man. When the latter em-

[Column 2]
erged from the waves the salt brine dried
upon his skin and instead of being a black
man he was perfectly white. (Laughter.)
This transformation, Porter Rockwell de-
clared to be the first miracle performed in
the valley. (Renewed merriment.) But
when he washed himself in fresh water the
salt disappeared and once more the white
man was black. (Laughter and applause.)
	On reaching here the Pioneers were im-
portuned to continue their journey to Cali-
fornia, where it was believed their condi-
tion would be bettered. President Young
said he knew he was in the midst of a des-
ert with his people, but that would make
no difference. They would build a temple,
a city and a commonwealth here. When
the first message flashed across the new
electric means of communication the words
were, “What has God wrought?” and so
regarding the declaration of President
Young the speaker felt constrained to say,
‘‘What has God wrought?” in these
mountain vales that are now cities and
towns bedecked with temples of worship,
school houses, colleges and the various
conditions of an advanced civilization.
	Here President Woodruff paused briefly
and then said: "Wondrous changes have
come to this land and people since the arri-
val of the Pioneers, and I want to say that
the changes, progress and increase will be
just as great during the next fifty-one years
as during a similar period that has just
closed. The destiny of this people is be-
fore heaven and earth and nothing can stay
it."
	President Woodruff said he would speak
of one incident and only one connected
with the journey of the Pioneers hither. It
was suggested to him by the presence on
the stand of W. C. A. Smoot who was the
youngest teamster in the advance guard. It
occurred near the Platte river. One day
the little band of home and liberty seekers
were startled by seeing to the west directly
ahead of them, what appeared to be a great
black cloud close to the earth. To their
amazement they discovered on approaching
nearer that it was not a cloud, but a solid,
mighty, moving body of buffalo, probably
the greatest herd that had ever assembled
in the western world. There was great
danger of being crushed to death by the
mighty horde. What to do was a problem.
But after consultation a wedgelike column
was thrown out and an opening effected in
the herd. Among this animated mass of
the monarchs of the mountains and plains
they struggled on for their own personal
salvation for three days and nights. At one
time Brother Smoot lost a horse which
got mixed up with the buffalo which were
traveling not in a scattered condition but
just as close as animals could crowd togeth-
er. For a time it seemed as though the
loss would be permanent, but after a while
the horse was recovered.
	"It always was,” said President Wood-
ruff, in his closing reference to the incident,
“a mystery to me why that great body of
buffalo should have gathered together and
marched from its roaming place on the
slopes of the Rocky Mountains and moved
down on to the Platte. It never occurred
before; it never occurred since, and will
never occur again.”
	As a finale to his address President
Woodruff said he felt that it was his duty
to give President Brigham Young the
credit of having laid the foundation of this
city and state. The streets of Salt Lake

[Column 3]
City, for instance, were the widest and 
most beautiful of any city on the earth. In
the historic city of Jerusalem two loaded
camels found it difficult to pass each other.
In this city thirty of these caravans of the
desert could do so. President Young be-
lieved in fulfilling the commandments of
God. He believed in doing things well.
That was a characteristic of the Pioneers,
no matter what it cost. For instance, a
mountain was moved at a cost of a half
a million dollars for a foundation for the
Manti Temple.

	Congressman Wm. H. King spoke, after a
quartette "When the swallows homeward
fly.” The speaker believed somewhat in
hero-worship and added "All Hail to such
men as Brigham Young, Wilford Woodruff,
Orson Pratt, Erastus Snow and George Q.
Cannon for what they have wrought;” and
he closed his eloquent tribute to the Pion-
eers with the expression: "Today all hon-
or and adoration should be given to Wilford
Woodruff, the Pioneer of pioneers.” Judge
C. C. Goodwin spoke last and was, as usual,
very humorous.
	President George Q. Cannon offered the
dedicatory prayer which was in that happy
spirit which goes to the hearts of the audi-
ence. This was followed by three cheers
for the Pioneers and also three cheers for
the Mayor and City Council who had been
instrumental in having this spot of conse-
crated ground set apart for the benefit of
the people.


EDITORIAL NOTES.

	Saturday evening, July 9, we had a
pleasant evening with Mrs. Hatch of Dallas,
Texas, and her brother and sister, Mr. and
Miss Buck, of Laporte, Indiana. The
young ladies are accomplished musicians.
Miss Buck not only sings and plays but
composes music.. Vacation Warbles and
other pieces of her own composition were
admirably rendered on the piano from
memory, and she sang by request, Sweet-
heart, Last Night, and The Sweetest Story
Ever Told. The young lady is also an
artist and excels in figure painting. The
other sister is quite gifted also and both are
very interesting.

	Mrs. J. E. Bamberger gave a fine lunch-
eon to a few ladies (about fifteen) on July
15,at her handsome residence in this city. As
we could not be present to see we are not
quite able to give the details, but have been
nformed it was a very pleasant party.
Among the guests were Mrs. Kate Tannatt
Woods of Boston, a distinguished visitor to
the city; Mrs. Amelia F. Young, Mrs. Sol.
Seigel, Mrs. Henry Seigel, Mrs. Simon
Bamberger, Mrs. Hanueuer, Mrs. W. P
Lynn, Mrs. C. E. Allen, Mrs. John Q.
Cannon and others. And certainly we
know Mrs. Bamberger to be a most genial
and entertaining hostess.
	We had the great pleasure of having
Mrs. Kate Tannatt Woods of Boston, at
our house for a few hours on Monday,
July 18, and also Mrs. W. P. Lynn of this
city, besides members of our own family
whom we wished should meet Mrs. Woods,
who is certainly one of the most interesting
conversationalists we have ever met, and as
she has traveled extensively and has known
so many celebrities, and besides has lived
in a most historic part of New England
near Salem, Danvers, Cambridge, Andover,


Amesbury, Haverhill and other notable
places in close proximity, how could she
help being a celebrity herself, if she par-
took of her surroundings. Sometime we
shall tell our readers more about this intel-
lectual woman, who every year writes a
book for children.
	Mrs. Kate Waller Barrett, of Washing-
ton, D. C., paid a brief visit to our city, ar-
riving on the 25th of July; we had met her
at the National Council in Washington in
1895, and she brought a letter of introduc-
tion to President George Q. Cannon. Mrs.
Barrett is National Superintendent of the
Florence Crittenden Homes, and is deeply
interested in the work; she is a very fasci-
nating woman in conversation, decidedly
clever and brilliant, has traveled extensive-
ly and knows many celebrities in our own
and foreign lands; has visited Japan, the
Holy Land and even Africa as well as near-
ly all the European countries, including
Spain; has been presented at four European
courts. We had an exceedingly interest-
ing visit with her during her short sojourn
and regretted very much she could not re-
main in the city longer.
	On Thursday July 7, we had the pleasure
of a short visit and chat at our home with
Mrs. Mary S. Gist of Washington, D. C.,
whom we have known through the N. W.
P. A. for some years. She is in many re-
spects a remarkable woman, has been fore-
most in good works at the headquarters
where many enterprises are originated, and
has known much of public affairs in the
capital of the United States, is a newspaper
correspondent and engaged in many chari-
table societies. She was a delegate from the
National Press Association to the Biennial
at Denver, came on here and spoke at the
Mothers’ Congress and was greatly delight-
ed with what she saw and heard while in
our city. It is gratifying to have intellect-
ual people come among us who are free
from prejudice and can see the right side of
things. 
	On Friday, May 15, we had the pleasure
of attending the Relief Society conference
of Utah Stake held in Provo, accompanied
by Mrs. Sarah J. Cannon. The president,
Mrs. Mary John, and her counselors, Mrs.
Marilla M. Daniels and Mrs. Deborah Bil-
lings, were on the stand also Mrs. Carol B.
Pratt, the very efficient secretary. The
minutes of the previous conference held at
Pleasant Grove were excellent. A com-
mittee of ladies had prepared an elaborate
lunch in the basement of the old tabernacle
and a hundred or more women partook of
the bounteous repast waited upon by sweet
young maidens, the affair was very enjoy-
able. President David John was present
in the afternoon and made a few remarks,
encouraging the Society in its good work.
Representatives were present from most of
the settlements in the county and gave
good reports.
	The Maine Memorial services in the
Tabernacle in this city, Sunday July 24,
were very impressive. The music by the
choir of hundreds of voices and the prayer
offered by President Angus M. Cannon
were in keeping with the spirit of the occa-
sion recognized. The speakers, Senator
Frank J. Cannon and Representative Wm.
H. King, were both familiar with the sub-
ject of the destruction of the ill-fated Maine
and of the condition of Cuba and with
Spanish cruelty, and their remarks were
fitting and eloquently earnest. The music
of the great organ lent significance to the
ceremonies and had it not been the Sabbath
and in a sacred place of worship, no doubt
the speakers and singers would have been
greeted with tremendous applause. Presi-
dent George Q Cannon offered the benedic-
tion which was a fitting tribute to the mem-
ory of the martyrs of the Maine. An orig-
nal poem entitled “Columbia” was given
by the author himself in a very pleasing
manner, and a collection was taken to as-
sist in erecting a suitable monument to the
memory of the martyrs who found an un-
timely grave in the briny deep, which was
a decidedly new departure from the estab-
lished custom of the Latter-day Saints who
do not solicit means from a congregation.
As there was a large congregation it is
hoped that a considerably large sum was
raised here and if also elsewhere in the State
Utah will have contributed a good share to
this laudable enterprise.
	The Primary Associations of Salt Lake
Stake under the auspices of the officers of
the Stake board and committee had an ex-
cursion to Saltair on Tuesday July 19, which 
was a very pleasant affair. Presidents Wil-
ford Woodruff and George Q. Cannon and
wife, also Counselors Jos. E. Taylor and
wife and C. W. Penrose and wife, Prof.
Karl G. Maeser and wife and some others
were guests of the party. The committee
had decorated the table beautifully and the
repast was daintily arranged and partaken
with a relish such as the salt air gives.
After lunch there were a few exercises by
the children and remarks suited to the time
and place were made by Presidents Wood-
ruff and Cannon. The children acquitted
themselves well, the flag drill, the dancing
by two wee small ones, the Highland fling
and the recitation by little Miss Penrose
Jones were all good. The singing of the
children under the direction of Brother
Bassett was good and patriotic. America
and The Star Spangled Banner always
thrills an audience and more especially in a
time like the present when the people are
already enthused. Much credit is due
those who had the training of the little ones
and to all who in any way contributed to
make the affair so pleasant and successful.
Success to the Primary and those who are
laboring in its interest, seeking to train the
children of this people in the paths of vir-
tue, truth and honesty.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]
OFFICERS MEETING,
	The officers’ meeting of the general con-
ference was held in the Assembly hall in
this city April 9, 1898, President Zina D.
H. Young presiding. Meeting opened
with singing “O, my Father.” Prayer
was offered by Dr. R. B. Pratt. Singing
“We thank Thee O, God for a prophet.”
	The president said, “We are all happy
and gratified to be able to meet together to
see each others faces, and especially are we
glad to see those from a distance whom we
seldom see, and I thank my Heavenly
Father that we enjoy so many blessings.
I would like to hear from Sister Rhoda
Hinman from Canada.”
	Sister Hinman said, “We have the Holy
Spirit with us. I remember when the Re-
lief Society was first organized; this work is
increasing from North to South and from
East to West.” Sister Hinman made
other interesting remarks.

[Column 2]
	Bannock Stake was reported by Sister
Susan Watts Burton, Bingham by the
president, Emma J. Bennett, who said that
in traveling through the Stake although
they traveled always by team no accident
had ever happened except last summer
when they had a runaway and six or seven
of them were bruised but that was all, felt
they had been preserved by an over-ruling
power. President Emily G. Cluff gave an
excellent account of the Hawiian sisters,
their industry, energy, and faithfulness.
	Counselor Kate L. Paxman of Juab Stake
spoke of the nice comfortable houses they
had in Nephi, and when they met in their
halls it seemed to her they were as sacred
as a temple, and said what excellent times
they had together among the sisters, and
also what support the presiding brethren in
the Stake gave them and how united they
were. 
	President Lydia Rich of Morgan Stake,
in representing said, “We have good sisters
and we have faith, it has been very effect-
ive in cases of sickness, angels are with us
and we feel their influence. We had diph-
theria in Morgan and we fasted and prayed
and the destroyer’s influence was stayed,
not another case broke out, the blessing of
the Lord has attended us all the day long.
	President Melissa Murdock of Charleston
Relief Society, spoke for Wasatch Stake,
referred to the death of Stake President
Emma Brown and stated that it was ex-
pected that another president would be ap-
pointed soon, thought all was going on
very well with the sisters in the Stake.
	President Jane S. Richards said in Weber
Stake the sisters were trying to do well,
to live peacably and love one another, they
had a good time at a late conference in the
ward where she lived and they bad many
good meetings and blessings, she enjoyed
working for the needy and considered the
labors and duties of the Society were spirit-
ual help, made many encouraging remarks
and felt we were greatly blessed of the
Lord.
	Counselor Annie E. Webster of Parowan
Stake, realized the greatness of the work
and that it was necessary we should be ex-
emplary in our labors and try to impress it
upon the mothers to teach correct princi-
ples in the home, to reckon with themselves
when night comes, go over the
events of the day, referred to her own child-
hood and early training.
	President Zina Young spoke on the silk
industry, told the sisters not to leave a
stone unturned in the silk business, set out
the cuttings of the mulberry trees, this
would help the children of the future; there
was no country in the world better adapted
to silk raising than this, sericulture would
give the women and children something to
do, it is an industry we can excel in and
give a great many employment, if we seek
good gifts they will be given to us, the gift
of wisdom if we pray for it. Sister Rhoda
Hinman’s mother, Mother Chase was set
apart by the Prophet Joseph to administer
to the sick and was one of the first in this
dispensation to attend to this ordinance; our
sisters should carry home from this confer-
ence the good word like a laurel or a sheaf
of grain to bless others with the teachings;
we must love each other and bless each
other also, we have a great mission, let us
have the spirit of kindness. “I was glad to
hear from those who are buildings houses,
we need houses of our own, we ought to
have a three days conference so that all


could speak and then we might be greatly 
benefited."
	President Sarah M. Kimball felt very 
much pleased to be present and to listen to
the teachings and to learn of the three
days' meetings, felt like Sister Zina about
having a house of our own, knew the sis-
ters had means. She had a different exper-
ience from some and her work had been dif-
ferent, but she believed she had a key to 
success in a measure and she would like to
give this key to the sisters in the Society,
it was the law of tithing; referred to a time
when a call was made, and she thought
how could she pay tithing, but the way
was opened and it was very peculiar; felt 
if one made a personal sacrifice the Lord
would bless one, and added that her exper-
ience had been pay your tithes and offerings
and the Lord will bless you. Sister Kimball
spoke of her acquaintance with the Prophet
Joseph in early days and said many excel-
lent things.
	President B. W. Smith urged the young-
er women as well as the older to join the
Relief Society said we should yet become
great and spread out in the land, prayed the
Lord to bless each and every one assembled,
talked of the days of Joseph the Prophet
and the good things he had said concerning
the Society, wanted us all to strive to do
good, to bless the sick, and comfort them,
say our prayers and live right and we
would be satisfied.
	A few closing remarks were made by the
president of love and blessing to all, the
congregation joined in singing "Praise God 
from whom all blessings flow," and confer-
ence was adjourned for six months. Prayer
was offered in the gift of tongues by Presi-
dent M. I. Horne, interpreted by Sister
Zina and Dr. E. S. Barney.
				E. B. WELLS, Sec.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


IN MEMORIAM.

ELEN DAVIES HUGHES.
	Elen Davies Hughes, wife of F. D. Hughes,
was born in South Wales December 10, 1841, and
died in Salt Lake City May 22, 1898 She em-
braced the Gospel i[-] her youth, belonged to the
Swansea branch and was noted for her integrity
and her faithful attendance to her meetings, was
strict in paying her tithing and always contributed
of her scanty means to every call made upon the
branch of which she was a member. She emi-
grated to Utah over thirty y[-]ars ago and has re-
sided in the 11th ward ever since. Her record
in that ward as a faithful Saint, wife and mother
were e[-]aborated upon by the brethren in their
inspired remarks at the funeral obsequies. In
short, she accepted every principle the Lord re-
vealed, inspired her children with faith in God
and passed away full of faith. It is a great con-
solation to her children (five of whom a[-]e living)
to look back upon her past life, and I trust they
will follow in her footsteps. She way va[-]iant in
testimony, patient and humble in a[-]l [-]rials and
tribulations. She has fought the good fight, she
has kept the faith and wi[-]l come forth in the
morning of the resurrection clothed with glory
and eternal life.
						H. D. HUGHES.
RESOLUTIONS OF RESPECT.
	Whereas, Our Heavenly Father has s[-]en fit in
His wisdom to allow our worthy and esteemed
sister, Agnes A. McDonald, president of the Re-
lief Society of Colonia Garcia Mexico, to be
ruthlessly murdered by the hands of foul assassins
for reason unknown to us, be it
	Resolved, That we the members of the Garcia
Relief Society extend our sympathy to the be-
reaved husband and children in this their sad
hour of trial. 
	Resolved, That the willing and faithful aid that
she exercised in our Relief Society by service,
contribution and counsel will be held in grateful
remembrance.
	Resolved, That her removal will leave a shadow


that will be deeply realized by the members of
this organization. 
	Resolved that we strive to carry out her wise 
counsel, follow her noble example and cherish in 
our hearts that love for her which she so truly 
won. 
	Resolved that we express our hope that even 
so great a loss to us all may be over-ruled for 
good y Him who doeth a[-]l things well. 
	Resolved that one copy of t e e resolutions be 
sent to the bereaved husband, one be placed on 
the Society record and one be sent to the “Ex-
ponent” for publication.
BARBARA WHETTEN,
WEALTHY SHEFFER,
Committee. 

		JANET WATSON MORRIS. 
	Sister Janet Wa son Morris was born in Ren-
frewshire, Scotland, July 28, 1842. S e moved 
with her parents when a child to Glasgow, where 
in 1864 she embraced the Gospel through oppo-
sition on the part of her family. In 1866 she left 
her home and her native land and emigrated to 
Utah, coming by ox team, and endured the dis- 
comforture of the journey with her characteristic 
cheerfulness and willingness to embrace all the 
principles the Lord revealed. 
	She was married to Robert Morris, now Bis op 
of the 11th ward, on December 21, 1867. She was 
the mother of six children, two sons and four 
daughters, her two sons having preceded her to 
the spirit world. 
	A few years ago she visited her native land 
where she succeeded in allaying much prejudice 
concerning Mormonism  She was a first and 
wise counselor in the 11 h ward Relief Society, 
which position she was appointed to fil on the 
7th of June, 1894. She was afflicted for years 
with rheumatism but bore her suffering cheerfully 
She bore a strong testimony to the truth of the 
work of God and was ever ready to do her duty
and to stand up for the principles of right. She 
has e rned the crown th t is awaiting her and wi l
come forth in the morning of the first resurrec-
tion. A large concourse followed her remains to 
the cemetery, the Relief Society going in the
“Utah.” May we all meet her again on the other 
side.
				LIZZIE LEEKER, Sec R. S.


RELIEF SOCIETY BRANCHES.

		NEW ORGANIZATIONS.
	A letter recently received from Bingham
July 15, 1898, states that a Relief Society
was organized at that place with Sister
Martha E. Thompson, president; Sisters
Eliza J. McNeil and Mary Hairt, counselors;
Sister Harriet S Jones, secretary. The sis-
ters were set apart on April 24, 1898. 
						H. S. JONES.

		SAN DIEGO, CAL.
	The San Deigo branch of the Church of
Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints having
increased to such a goodly number Presi-
dent E. H. Nye of the California mission
and Conference President G. F. Harding
effected a Ladies Relief Society, May, 11,
1898.
	The following officers were set apart un-
der the hands of the above named presi
dents and Elders F. B. Platt, J. A. Love-
less and W. E. Stoker, traveling Elders in
San Diego City: Sister Amelia Jewell, 
president; Sister Mary N. Gabrielson, first
counselor; Sister Ida E. Hunt, second coun-
selor; Sister Louisa Edwards, secretary;
Sister Anna Buhorn, treasurer; Sisters
Louisa Swanson and Adelia C. Kingsbury,
teachers.
	Three meetings have been held during
the month, each meeting was well attend-
ed and the Spirit of God was enjoyed. The
work is being carried on very successfully.
The prospects for the future are bright for
accomplishing a good work.
	LOUISA EDWARDS, Sec.


R. S. REPORTS 
		MARICOPA STAKE. 
	Report of Relief Society Stake conference 
held June 16, 1898. The sisters compris-
ing the Maricopa Stake Relief Society held 
their quarterly conference in the Society 
hall with First Counselor Francelle E. Rob-
son presiding. President Mabel A. Hakes
was with us but was in very feeble health, 
also Counselor Sarah N. Johnson who was 
very poorly in health, also Stake President 
C. R. Hakes and Counselor Henry C. 
Rogers and other leading sisters 
	Meeting was opened with the usual ex-
ercises. All the wars were represented in 
good condition generally, showing a good
spirit among the sisters. The topics dwelt on 
were for our general good, storing grain 
now that we are just reaping a bounteous 
harvest, leading exemplary lives, and many 
testimonies were borne by the sisters. Two 
Lamanite sisters bore strong testimonies to 
the truth of the Gospel, which was very in-
teresting to all. These sisters, of whom 
there are quite a number, were very sincere, 
they felt glad to have their names in rec- 
ord, as they saw the secretary taking notes 
of what was said. They speak through an 
interpreter. President Hakes and Counsel-
or Rogers exhorted the sisters to come out 
to meeting oftener, also spoke of the duties 
of the Relief Society, said we should first 
seek the Kingdom of God and all things 
will be added. All felt that we had had a 
good time together. 
	Meeting adjourned for three months. 
Benediction by H. C. Rogers. 
	ANNA E. FULLER, Sec. 

		SANPETE STAKE. 
	Minutes of the Relief Society conference 
of the Sanpete Stake of Zion held at Mt. 
Pleasant, June 10, 1898, President M. A. 
Hyde presiding. Singing “Redeemer of
Israel.” Prayer by Sister Neilsen. Sing-
ing "Come, come ye Saints.
	Opening remarks were made by Presi-
dent Hyde. She spoke of a meeting she 
had attended in Salt Lake City and de- 
livered a kindly message of love from Aunt 
Zina. Referred to the progress of the 
work as reported by the missionaries. 
Minutes of the previous conference were 
read and approved. Verbal reports were 
given by representatives from the following 
wards: Spring City, Ephraim north and 
south wards, Manti north and south wards 
Mt. Pleasant, Chester, Centerfield, Fayette; 
Milburn, Indianola. Moroni, Gunnison
Freedom and Mayfield represented by let-
ter, all represented except Sterling and
Wales.
	President M. A. Hyde related a case of 
healing by faith which had recently come 
under her observation, also spoke on the 
word of wisdom. Singing “Earth with
her ten thousand flowers.” Benediction
by Elder Moroni Jensen. 
	Afternoon session:—Singing “How firm
a foundation.” Prayer by Elder Edward 
Cliff. Singing “Our God we raise to
Thee.”
	President Hyde deplored the fact that the 
gifts of the Gospel were not more exer- 
cised. Said that Elders had come to the 
Relief Society meeting in order to hear the 
gift of tongues and the interpretation there-
of, in order that they might testify to these 
things to an unbelieving world when called 
on missions. Exhorted the sisters to be


diligent in watching over their sons as
well as their daughters.
	President M. F. C. Morrison of Mt.
Pleasant was much interested in the wel-
fare of the young people, told the mothers
to gather the children together and instruct
them and find out what company they keep.
	Stake Counselor Abigail Shoemaker
hoped the sisters were striving to live their
religion. Thought both old and young
should take an interest in the meetings.
	Stake Counselor A. L. Cox was pleased
to hear the good reports from the various
branches of the Society and also at the
good spirit which had prevailed during the
conference. Made reference to the present
war with Spain and the judgment which
would eventually be poured out on that
nation in consequence of centuries of in-
tolerance and oppression. Said we should
be a progressive people, and should seek
for the spirit of discernment that we might
be able to understand the signs of the times,
also referred to the storing of wheat, said it
was a sacred trust to the sisters and that it
should not be used for any other purpose
than that for which it was gathered, that is
for a day of famine.
	Sister Peel told how much she appreciat-
ed the blessings of the Lord to His people
during the time of poverty and trial.
	Brother Cliff had rejoiced and been built
up while listening to the remarks of the
sisters. He spoke of the war and said that
all of our boys who responded to their
country’s call and who enlist with the
right spirit would be preserved as were the
sons of Helaman.
	President Acord of the Stake Primary
desired to have the parents take an inter-
est in the children and visit their meetings.
Said they should not think it was of little
importance.
	Singing “O, say what is truth.” Bene-
diction by Brother Hafeau. Conference
adjourned for three months.
	Since the conference the Stake counselors
have visited all the branches in the
Stake and found them all in good condition
and alive to their duties.
	A.L. COX, Act. Cor. Sec.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]
NOTES AND NEWS.

	The bill raising the age of protection for 
girls in the District of Columbia to eighteen 
passed the U. S. House of Representatives 
before adjournment. 
	Until the remonstrants produce a second 
Clara Barton, it will remain true that the 
most effective type of patriotism has come 
from the ranks of the suffragists. 
	On “Club Woman’s Day,” at the Trans-
Mississippi Exposition, Mrs. Kate Tannatt 
Woods of Salem, read a paper on “Club
Courtesies,” and Mrs. Ellen A. Richardson 
of Boston, spoke on “The Keystone of the
Educational Arch.”
	A Law Committee consisting of Mrs. 
Halsted Smith, Rome; Mrs. Frances Akin, 
Cartersville; Mrs. Walter Hill, Macon; and 
Mr. E. Cameron Reed, Atlanta, have fin-
ished their arduous task of making extracts 
from the code of Georgia upon the law of 
the State regarding woman’s legal status. 
The work is well done and is receiving uni-
versal praise wherever the pamphlet is re-
ceived. 
	In England the woman suffrage cause 
has lost two of its oldest friends in Parlia-

[Column 2]
ment. The Hon. C. P. Villiers, “the
Father of the House,” who died at the age 
of ninety-six, on Jan. 16, had never failed 
to vote for the measure from 1870 to 1892,
when he came at great personal inconven-
ience to record his vote for Sir Albert Rol-
lit’s Bill. Mr. George Dixon, M. P. for 
Edgbaston, has also voted since 1870, but 
not so frequently, as he was absent from 
Parliament for several years. 
	The Woman’s National Cuban League 
at Washington, which was an active factor 
in furthering the Cuban cause last fall, has 
reorganized, adopted the old charter and re-
elected its former director-general, Mrs. 
Clara Bell Brown, and its vice director-
general, Mrs. Rosa Louisa Townsend, a 
great granddaughter of one of the signers 
of the Declaration of Independence. The 
purpose of the reorganized league is to give 
aid and comfort to American soldiers and 
sailors. 
	The army hospital ship, Relief, which 
has been fitting out at New York during 
the past month, is the most perfect vessel 
of its kind in the world. As compared 
with the Solace, the navy hospital ship, the 
Relief is superior in structure, speed, ac- 
commodation and equipment. Its berths
for sick and wounded number 1,300, and 
nearly a hundred additional patients can be 
cared for in an emergency. The ship will 
carry a crew under contract, not belonging 
either to the army or navy. The nurses 
will number ten male and eight female
The former are from Mills Training School 
in New York City, and the women from 
John Hopkins Hospital Training School at 
Baltimore. Ex. 
	Mrs. M. E. W. Sherwood tells this anec-
dote in her new volume of reminiscences,
“Here and There and Everywhere.”
“I was present at many dinners when 
Willis was the life of the company, and al-
though I did not hear the famous repartee 
of the Washington dinner so often recorded, 
I will record it here. It was Mrs. Gales, I 
think, who at one of her own dinners wrote 
a card to her niece at the other end of the 
table: “Don’t flirt so with Nat Willis.”
She was talking vivaciously herself to Mr. 
Cambell. Willis replied:
“Dear aunt don’t attempt my young feelin[-]s to 
	trammel,
Nor strain at a Nat while you swallow a Cam-
	bell.”						Ex.


HOW TO CLEAN WINDOWS.

	In the annual work of spring cleaning
there is nothing more important to have
properly done than the washing and polish-
ing of the windows. Unlike other branch-
es of house cleaning, a dull cloudy day is
best for window cleaning as experienced
housekeepers say it cannot be so well done
when the sun is shining on the glass.
When ready to begin the glass should be
well wiped with a soft, dry cloth to free it
from dust, then should be quickly washed
with warm water to which powdered borax
is added and then dried. To polish the
glass after cleaning, fill a small muslin bag
with whitening to which a little powdered
borax is added, and dust thickly over; then
wipe off with a soft cloth and rub with old
newspapers Windows thus washed will
be beautifully clean and bright and will
keep clean a long time.
