[Column 1]
	ONLY A GIRL. 

	BY MRS. A. E. N. R. 
Close the door carefully, muffle the tread, 
Drop the white curtains ’round the white bed; 
A pale mother’s sleeping, aye give her rest, 
See the fresh rosebud upon her white breast. 
She has struggled with pain, she has wrestled 
	with death;
Her’s is the victory! let not a breath 
Awaken her slumber, hark! there's a tread, 
Nearer and nearer approaching the bed;
Manly his bea[-]ing—yea noble his mien; 
Lowly he bends the fair sleepers between;
Lifts the frail floweret with womanly care, 
Breathlessly gazing, his lips part in prayer! 
No! there’s a chill in the a[-]bient air, 
Each word falls distinctly and painfully slow, 
Curdling and freezing the blood in its flow;
“Its only a girl ?”—a hush as of death 
For a moment suspended each listener’s breath;
n the pause—the pale sleeper uplifted her eyes—
“I must have been dreaming,” she said with
	surprise,
“I thought that a cold hand of iron touched my
	heart,
While hard, cruel words like a poisonous dart, 
Pierced my soul to the core; I sprang for my
	babe!
‘Its only a girl!’ were the words I heard said, 
And Elmer! Oh Elmer! that voice was like thine;
That hand—angels spare me!—once warmly
	clasped mine, 
As you called me more precious than ruby or
	pearl,
And yet, it was when I was only a girl! 
If a girl is thus dear, then the mother and wife 
To every true man is as dear as his life!”
She clasped her cold hands o’er her hot throbbing
	brow—
The blood had all rushed to that citadel now;
Then her words, quick and scathing, burned into 
	the soul!
Emotion swayed reason beyond her control—
“Its only a girl!”—O man in thy strength, 
Know that God measures souls by their depth 
	not their length; 
The streamlet may wind over miles of fair earth, 
Yet bear on its bosom [-]o proud ship of worth; 
A man may hold kingdoms, and nations control; 
What is that to the birth of one beautiful soul? 
The germ in your strong arms, unfolded with
	care,
May, like Harriet Hosmer or Rosa Bonheur, 
Move the world by their art, or lull it to rest 
With poesy's magic, and balm of the blest. 
The mission of motherhood! Man do you dare 
With sneers stain the sa[-]ctum sanctorum of 
	prayer? 

[Column 2]
This Holy of Holies—this mightiest dower? 
Dare to scoff at the sex in which lies this power?
Ah, where were the Monarch, the Duke and the
	Earl,
Had not each a mother—once "only a girl!”
And whence came thy being, and all the proud
	van 
You marshalled in battle—yes every man? 
The magnet that led them through storm and 
	through strife, 
Was a mother, a sister, a sweetheart, or wife, 
Each closely enshrined in his heart like a pearl;
And yet each fair image was “only a girl!”
It was only a girl that Deity chose
To incarnate the Christ; the story in prose 
Sweeps down through the ages like stars through 
	the night, 
To illumine the world with its God given light. 
It was only frail woman that wept at the tomb, 
And talked with the angels when Jesus had gone;
And woman that bore the glad tidings to man 
That Christ, the Beloved, had risen again. 
Go to the reeking battlefields of yore
And read the records writ in human gore, 
Of woman’s valor, mercy, courage, love, 
And point me to one name that’s carved above 
The name of woman in such deeds as these, 
And I will pray to heaven on bended knees, 
That every child henceforth may be a boy;
That every father’s heart may leap with joy. 
But ere in scorn you breath “only a girl!”
Look, lest you cast aside the greater pearl.
		—Selected.


[Column 1]
	THE SILK INDUSTRY.

	SPRINGVILLE LETTER.

	Ages have demonstrated that immense
wealth has been produced by sericulture.
The Chinese and Japanese date back thou-
sands of years the existence of this wonder-
ful industry which has furnished the
most costly and ornamental clothing the
world has ever worn, even royalty itself
ows its richness of attire, its costliest adorn-
ment to a worm so forbidding to some of
our more refined ladies, as to become repul-
sive to the sight, to say nothing of the
touch. And yet this worm is the producer
of all the silk in the world.
	Only imagine for a single moment the
destruction of this insect, its removal from
earth forever, and what a loss to the attire
of the world this would be. No more silk
dresses, no ribbons, silk necktie or pocket-
handkerchief. Countless millions of wealth
would be cut off and the occupation of
millions of workers. Now I hope to arouse
an interest in every reader of the “Expon-
ent” for this great industry, by submitting
figures and facts.
	Presuming upon the number of forty
thousand families in Utah and that each
family raises only twenty pounds of cocoons.
The bounty alone on this would be $5.00 to
each family, which means $200,000 to the
State in bounty alone; but let us carry these
figures further. Twenty pounds of cocoons
would produce four pounds of reeled silk,
which at four dollars a pound means
$21.00 to each family, $840,000 to
the State. But if each family should
produce forty pounds, which can be done

[Column 2]
in every house that has a boy ten years old
to gather the leaves, this would give the
sum of $1,680,000 yearly.
	I trust this statement will appeal so
strongly to all that they will not allow this
golden opportunity to pass unheeded and
thus prevent employment to hundreds of
our sons and daughters. We have quality,
what we want is quantity to encourage
capital to establish mills.
	President Young predicted that this
would yet become one of the greatest in-
dustries of the country, and the experience
of the present year has proven the truth of
his words.
	Not only so, but once engaged in good
earnest it becomes fascinating, as I have
learned from my own experience, having
produced in my own house thirty-seven and
a half pounds of cocoons this season, which
netted us $9.30 in bounty alone.
	There were only two of us to do the
housework and the additional labor of at-
tending the worms was without hired help.
	I wish to add a caution about building
scaffolding against a wall. Build out so
that you can pass around, and be sure to
commence to hatch the eggs not later than
the last week in April. Our bounty to six
families in Springville this season was
$40.00.
	For further information apply to Mrs.
Margaret A. Caine, secretary of the Silk
Commission, also the pamphlet of the com-
mission. These are reliable guides. All
books and eggs are gratis. Every county
raising one hundred pounds of cocoons is
entitled to a person to teach them how to
reel silk.
			ELIZABETH PACKARD.

		ST. GEORGE LETTER.

	A few words in regard to the silk indus-
try of this country might be of interest to
your readers. During the early part of
this year Miss Horrocks of Salt Lake City,
under the direction of the Utah Silk Com-
misson, taught fifteen married and twenty-
one unmarried ladies who took a course in
reeling the silk upon the half dozen Japan-
ese reels recently purchased from Japan.
The most of them were delighted with the
work and made rapid progress.
	Last spring there were twenty-six fami-
lies engaged in silk culture in the county.
The product so far reported amounts to
three hundred and sixty-four pounds of
cocoons. These when reeled will make
about ninety pounds of reeled silk.
A good reeler can reel this silk at the rate
of half a pound per day. Within the last
year we have had about ninety yards of
silk woven; it was not of the best quality
owing to the lack of experienced weavers,
we are pleased to say, however, that we
have now secured the services of a skillful
weaver, and we hope in the near future to
have some silk that we can put before the
market.
	There are a great many mulberry trees
in St. George, and in most of the Dixie set-
tlements there are a great many; they make
rapid growth for shade trees. In
St. George Miss Horrocks estimated


[Column 1]
there was enough to feed the worms to
make a thousand pounds of cocoons, and
every year there will be a great increase in
the number set out, owing to the increase
in the water supply since we have the
cottonwood water in town. It is not much
trouble to raise the trees when you can
keep them watered.
	There are many men looking toward the
silk industry as a source of revenue and
with that end in view they expect to plant
mulberries by the acre, and in talking with
influential men on this subject they ex-
press a desire to know what to do to ad-
vance it the most. Of course the right
kind of mulberry tree is the first considera-
tion. There has been an increased interest
in the work since we have used the Japa-
nese reels, and also since we have had silk
woven. Of course we have had many diffi-
culties to contend with, but in beginning
on a small scale and getting the silk into
cloth it showed the people that we meant
business and they can see what can be ac-
complished with a little capital. Expert
reelers must be the first consideration in es-
tablishing factories. Now we have quite a
quantity of reeled silk on hand and a large
number of good reelers. All they want is
encouragement. They ask: “What will we
do with the silk when we get it reeled?”
If we had the necessary means to buy the
reeled silk from those who have produced it,
it would encourage them to push the work.
There is not much encouragement to work
unless they can get such pay as they can
use, for they need it. There are not many
who can afford to reel it and lay it away.
	A young lady from one of the settlements
sent me three quarters of a pound of reeled
silk, very beautiful, and wanted to sell it.
We have such chances as this one often.
We give this industry all the encourage-
ment we can and intend to while life lasts,
or until we see it the blessing and pride of
this country.
			ANN C. WOODBURY.
THE SILK INDUSTRY.
	So much has been said on this subject
that when requested to write I thought,
“What more can I add to what has already
been said and written?” Still if I can add
one word of encouragement to lead our
young people to see the value of this in-
dustry I cheerfully do so.
	How many times I have been asked the
question, “Does it pay to raise silk?” and
I always answer, “Yes, it certainly does.”
But there are some things to be remembered.
First of all we must have mulberry trees, it
is just as necessary that we should have
trees to raise silk, as it is that we should
have land to raise grain; and, by the way,
this fall or next spring will be a good time
to plant trees, and let every one make it a
special point to plant several and in this
way a great deal would be done towards
advancing the cause of the silk industry.
	The Pioneer Nursery Company will be
glad to furnish mulberry trees from five to
fifteen feet high at prices from ten to thirty
cents each.
	After our trees have grown large enough,
which will be about three years, let each
one get right to work. Get just enough
eggs so that the worms can be well fed and
cared for, and in a short time you will be
rewarded by seeing them spin you a nice
lot of beautiful cocoons. The next step is
the reeling; this has been a great drawback
in the past, and how many days have

[Column 2]
spent in trying to get some one to reel my
silk and at last have it rendered unfit for
first class silk. But this is now all over-
come; through the earnest efforts of the Silk
Commission we are now enabled to obtain
reels and the law provides for a person to
teach the reeling at the expense of the State.
There is also bounty of twenty-five cents
on each pound of cocoons raised, which has
been provided by law and ought to arouse
an interest in silk culture.
	We have people in this State who under-
stand the different branches of this indus-
try, such as dyeing, cleansing etc., and as
soon as the demand is sufficient we hope
machinery will be obtained to make the
work lighter
	And now we do hope that those men and
women who are elected to represent us both
in Washington and in the State, whatever
their political belief, will appreciate the
efforts that have been made in this direction
and will give us aid, not only by their in-
fluence but will seek to have us helped in a
financial way. Let each one, no matter
what his or her political belief, seek to get
good men and women in office, who will
work to build up this important branch of
work and other home industries in the
State.
	Let me urge the mothers and daughters
to see that every mulberry tree in the State
is utilized in producing silk. Even now
we can produce a quantity and quality that
is surprising. Speaking of the latter, I
wish every one could see the difference in
the imported silk and our own. I had the
privilege recently of seeing pieces of the
imported and the homemade silk compared,
and really the imported hardly looked like
silk in comparison to our own homemade.
	Let us be more united in our efforts to
develop this industry, for it will be the
little that each one does that will make up
the whole.
			MARIA ZUNDELL.
SILK CULTURE PAST AND PRESENT IN
SANPETE.
	Since Utah has become a state the sub-
ject of home industry and especially seri-
culture has received more attention than
for some years previous, and with judicious
protection and encouragement there is no
good reason why it should not become a
source of wealth and independence to the
people of these mountain valleys.
	Owing to advanced age and infirmities I
am unable to take an active part in the
further development of this important in-
dustry, but if my experience in the past in
this line will be of any benefit to the young-
er generation I shall feel that I am not liv-
ing in vain.
	From my early childhood I have been
perfectly familiar with this business. In
my native state (Connecticut) the scanty
income which the farmers were enabled to
wring from the worn out soil was largely
supplemented by the proceeds of a small
cocoonery which nearly every family main-
tained in their own house. An unused,
vacant, airy chamber furnishing the neces-
sary accomodation for the worms with very
little expense, and very much more profit
than where large sums have been invested
in building expensive structures for this
purpose. So far as my experience goes
both in New England and Illinois, large
cocooneries have invariably resulted in loss
and failure.
	In the year 1873 I again engaged in silk

[Column 3]
culture at my home in Manti as an experi-
ment, and my success far excelled my anti-
cipations. I have demonstrated to my en-
tire satisfaction that the climate and atmos-
phere of Utah is far more favorable to the
health and development of the silk worm
than that of the eastern states. The dis-
ease superinduced by the continuous wet
weather does not exist in our dry climate,
and in fact the only obstacle in the way of
complete success is the present lack of suf-
ficient mulberry trees which can be easily
remedied wherever water is available. There
should be no lack of energy in this direc-
tion as an initial step in accomplishing
great results. But in this as in every other
undertaking patience and perseverance are
necessary to success. The silk worm sea-
son comes during the summer vacation
of the schools, thus giving the children
time and opportunity to pick the leaves and
assist in caring for the worms. If they are
familiar with them from infancy as I was
they do not feel the aversion toward them
that many of our young ladies manifest at
present. Also give the little ones to under-
stand that diligence on their part will be re-
warded when the silk is sold.
	In the year 1876 Sister E. R. Snow or-
ganized a silk association in Manti for San-
pete county, and for several years a con-
siderable amount of silk was produced in
Manti by Sister Maria Weibye and myself,
also in Ephraim by a Sister Peterson and
in Gunnison by Sister Helena Madsen.
All these sisters are deserving of much
credit for their pioneer labors in behalf of
silk culture. Sister Elizabeth Higgs has
also been indefatigable in her labors as silk
agent, canvassing the town and urging up-
on the sisters the immediate necessity of
planting mulberry trees.
	In one season I made sufficient silk for a
dress but it was unfortunately spoiled in
the weaving. Two years ago my grand-
daughter raised sufficient cocoons to obtain
$2.25 bounty. She also sold them to Mrs.
Margaret A. Caine for the Omaha Exposi-
tion. She is a teacher in the public schools
but she thinks she can raise silk during her
summer vacation. I hope that many more
young ladies may feel impressed to go and
do likewise. I feel that there is a great
future for this State if our people will only
be wise in their day and generation, and by
wise legislation and individual effort care-
fully utilize the elements within our reach,
which will tend to make us a prosperous
and independent people.
	Let us hope that our new legislature
will give this important subject the atten-
tion it deserves during the coming session.
Let our sisters realize that the silk indus-
try will open a new field of occupation for
their children, which will be the means of
adding many comforts to the homes of those
who are in straightened circumstances if
they avail themselves of the opportunities
offered. Let our young ladies see to it that
their adornment shall be the work of their
own hands and not the products of Babylon.
	And above all let us realize the purpose
for which we have come to this promised
land, and strive diligently to become the
self-sustaining people the Lord designs us
to be.
		REBECCA WAREHAM.
ENCOURAGING SILK CULTURE.
	One of the first acts of the legislature of
Utah after Statehood had been declared, was
the passing of a bill to encourage silk cul-


[Column 1]
ture in the new state. No act could have
been more commendable. In doing this
those who are the leaders of thought, de-
clared their interest in the welfare of the
people at large. They showed their belief
in a great principle that it is the duty of
the State to take the responsibility of en-
couraging industry, of assisting the isolated
and dependent to united action which shall
result to the good of all. The personnel of
the commission appointed by Governor
Wells to carry out the idea of the act of the
legislature were a guarantee that the inten-
tion of the bill would be faithfully adhered
to. Mrs. Zina D. H Young, Mrs. C. W.
Bennett, Mrs. M. A. Caine and two others,
one in St. George and one in Springville,
should be forever held in grateful remem-
brance by the people of Utah for the con-
scientious way in which they have executed
their trust. It is gratifying to note the
great increase of interest in the silk culture
in Utah from year to year since the silk
bill was passed. The increase of one hun-
dred dollars in bounties this last year
means a great increase in production. May
another year bring still greater increase.
	If the women of the State generally
will give as much time and labor to raising
cocoons as the women of the Silk Commis-
sion have given to the necessary administra-
tive work connected with the distribution of
eggs and testing the cocoons, in a few
years the silk industry will be on the road
to permanent establishment.
	It is interesting to go back in history
over the story of the growth of the produc-
tion of silk fabrics.
	The process of rearing the worms, reel-
ing the thread and weaving the cloth is
said to have been discovered in China, 2600
B. C. The secret of silk culture was
guarded most jealously by the Chinese. It
was death to reveal the process to a foreign-
er, and so perfectly was the will of the
government in this matter carried out, that
it was not until 555 A. D., that the world
east of China had any knowledge of the
precious art. At that time two Mesbian
monks brought silk worm eggs concealed
in their staves to the Emperor Justinian at
Constantinople. Silk culture immediately
became established in Europe.
	France has become the greatest silk pro-
ducing country because there the govern-
ment has done most to foster and protect
the industry. At one time silk workers
were exempt from taxation and every in-
ducement was offered to engage in the pro- 
duction of cocoons. The wisdom of this
national policy in France has been proved
by the result. At the present time in
France silk is manufactured at the value of
one hundred and twenty million francs an-
nually, and from 800,000 to 1,000,000 oper-
ators are employed. It is hardly too much
to say that the silk industry in France
makes the difference in that country be-
tween prosperity and the want of it.
	Efforts in America to establish silk cul-
ture have been numerous but spasmodic.
Georgia was established as a silk colony, but
the vicissitudes of those early colonists are
sufficient explanation of the failure of the
project. Before the war of the Revolution
many of the colonists were successfully en-
gaged in silk culture, but when the war be-
gan the industry was entirely suspended.
From 1825 to 1844 there was a great reviv-
al of interest in silk culture throughout the
country, but one of those inexplicable ex-
citements to which nations are subject, tem-

[Column 2]
porarily suppressed the industry. The
gambling in the stocks of mulberry cuttings
reached a point only equalled by the tulip
craze in Holland. Many men lost their en-
tire fortunes and every one was foolishly
turned from the idea of engaging in silk
culture.
	The people of Utah are quite familiar
with the history of sericulture in this State.
There has been a constant attempt on the
part of the leaders of the people to guide
them to industries of all kinds which shall
make them useful and prosperous. Silk
culture is an important industry to which.
Utah is well adapted by its climatic condi-
tions. What has already been done in silk
culture in Utah is a promise that great
things can be accomplished in the future.
The time should be near at hand when all
the silk required in the various cloth manu-
factories in Utah should be supplied at
home. The thousands of dollars then earned
will help to educate our children, build
comfortable houses, improve our roads and
eventually prove one of the foundation
stones upon which the prosperity of the
State shall rest.
	Let every woman who raises a few pounds
of cocoons in her little home feel that she
is a factor in a great enterprise which the
State is undertaking for the good of us all.
As she watches the wonderful mystery of
the assimilation of the fibers of the mul-
berry leaves and the spinning of the golden
web, may she feel that this wonderful pro-
cess of nature is to be made use of for com-
munity life. The perfect adaptability of
God’s plan of nature to the needs of man,
to my mind, has never been more clearly
indicated than in the development of the
silk industry. May the State continue to
foster it and patriotic women continually re-
spond to the efforts of the State.
			CORINNE W. ALLEN.


WOMANS EXPONENT


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


RED TAPE.

	[The following from a Chicago paper illustrates
the too frequent course of events in volunteer,
service.]
	He started to war in splendid shape,
	But they held him in with a piece of tape,
				Red tape.
	He fought with canister and grape
	As well as he could.They were tied with tape,
				Red tape.
	He lived on what he could rake and scrape
	For his regular grub was tied with tape,
				Red tape.
	And many and many a narrow escape 
	He had because he was hampered with tape, 
				Red tape.
	He sickened. They laid his wasted shape
	On a nice, soft cot that was made of tape,
				Red tape.
	He died. His form they proceeded to drape
	In a winding sheet that they made of tape,
				Red tape.
	Let not the symbol of mourning be crape;
	Let the door knob simply be wrapped with tape
				Red tape.


Miss Chanler, of New York, has received
great praise from Lieutenant Colonvi Nich-
olas Senn, chief of the operating staff of the
army who has just returned from Porto
Rico. He says the Red Cross nurse worked
night and day with untiring zeal minister-
ing to the sick and wounded soldiers. Miss
Chanler is a niece of Mrs. Julia Ward Howe.


EVOLUTION.

	“Blessed are they that hunger and thirst after
righteousness for they shall be filled.”
I walk in the radiant glory of a new-born, golden
	day,
As full of calm, fresh sunshine as the cloudless
	skies of May,
And my steps are light as Zephyr, for my hope is
	bright as morn,
And the glow of the day shall brighten in the
	light that is yet unborn.

In the vistas of ages vanished, in the tears and 
	groans and strife,
In the turbulent pulse [-]f the cosmos, in the 
	panting toil of life.
In the chaos all but formless, in the cloud that
	holds a rod,
In the dreary tread of ages I behold the hand
	of God.

Upward and ever upward His radiant beacons
	mount,
And the heart of the world is quickened, by the
	infinite sun it fount,
The Fount of the Infinite Father, so gentle and
	true and kind, 
Patiently striving and giving Himself to the
	darkened finite Mind.

For ages before man’s knowledge, I see from the
	lowly sod,
A white, wan face uplifted in a broken prayer to
	God;
So the prayer hath touched the heart of Him who
	is Joy and Love and Light,
And the sweet response lifts the lowly Heart up,
	up fiom the depths of Night.

Still on in the dim prospective, in the solemn
	march of time,
We read the story of Light and Love struggling
	up to a height sublime. 
Eternity looms before us: shall Humanity’s dream
	come true?
Ah, yes! For the trend of the Soul mounts on
	with deathless Life in view!

Though here in the shadows of earth-life our
	human strength grows faint,
Through sorrow and sin and darkness the crushed
	soul moans comp[-]aint,
Yet Truth never sleeps on the Tower, God’s
	forces of love march on,
And we shout our joy to the great wide world as
	the next black shade has gone.

Though our hearts may be chilled in the night-
	shades, where the saviors of men have stood,
Ever battling with souls undaunted, for the up-
	ward trend of God,
’Tis sweet to know that the irid east for the,
	famished heart holds more
Than even the glorious, purpled morn hath
	promised to hold in store.

And so I walk in a fairy dream with my spirit all
	aglow,
And joy in the light of a day that will come with
	the shadows all laid low.
For the Father’s hand is upon the helm and the
	heart of man will yield,
And evil shall cease for the soul of Christ shall
	garner the whitened field.
					RUBY LAMONT.
Circleville, Utah, Sep. 6, 1898.

chances are that the ticket would be
Theodore Roosevelt and Helen Gould.
	Miss Helen Gould is going about Camp
Wikoff finding sick soldiers who have no
home and no friends and sending them up
to Terrytown. where she has provided a
temporary hospital of her own.

SALT LAKE CITY OCTOBTR 1 & 15, 1898.


THE GENERAL CONFERENCE.

	The General Semi-Annual Conference
held this month commencing on Thursday
the 6th inst., and closing its sessions on
Sunday afternoon, October 9, was one of
the most important conferences in the his-
tory of the Church. The solemn and im-
pressive spectacle of the voting by the
several quorums of the priesthood and the
general assembly was calculated to awaken
the spiritual emotions of every member of
the Church and to make a lasting impres-
sion upon all who witnessed it.
	The sermons and instructions during the
conference have been particularly spiritual
in their character, although some temporal
matters have necessarily been brought to
the attention of the people in a forcible
way, which cannot but be beneficial in the
daily lives of those who are striving to
build up a great commonwealth, and to es-
tablish principles of economy, industry and
thrift, as shall prove to those who receive
the same their faith by their works.
	The great need of faith in God has been
one of the strong points in all the teaching
given, and the need of a living and abiding
testimony of the truth of the Gospel as re-
vealed in this last dispensation. The pros-
perity of the people in these valleys, the
abundance of good things of this life en-
joyed by those who have settled here in
Utah under such adverse circumstances,
the great work of gathering the Saints
from other lands, and many other subjects
were treated upon by the speakers, and the
future awaiting those who remained faith-
ful to their covenants entered into and obli-
gations resting upon them were plainly set
forth to the understanding of all present.
	The music has been of the highest class,
indeed everything has been done to uplift
and encourage the Saints, and one cannot
help feeling that those who came to be fed
with the bread of life have received each a
portion in due season and it has created a
feeling of confidence in the hearts of those who
hearkened and heard—that these who pre-
side here upon the earth do indeed have
communion with Him who rules and reigns
over the destinies of the children of men,
and that they know the mind and will of
the Lord from day to day and from time to
time concerning Zion. A feeling of sereni-
ty and of peace abides with the Saints
notwithstanding the turmoil, strife and up-
rising among the nations of the earth of
which we hear and read continually.


ANNOUNCEMENT.

	The annual executive sessions of the
National Council of Women of the United
States in Omaha will convene on the 24th
to the 29th of October (inclusive) under
the auspices of the Woman’s Department
of the Trans-Mississippi Exposition. The
rates from the West are more reasonable
than ever before, and it is desirable that
women who have means should educate
themselves in the Council idea which is
much more important, influential and far-
reaching than one might think who is not
thoroughly posted on the subject.
	The Relief Society and the Y. L. M. I.
A. are both connected with the National
Council, and every officer and also members
of the great organization should try to get
a proper understanding of the advantages
that occur to the associations through their
membership and standing with other wo-
men the world over.


EDITORIAL NOTES.

	There will be an entertainment given at
the handsome new residence of Mr. and
Mrs. O. J. Salisbury in this city in the inter-
est of the woman’s department of the George
Washington Memorial Fund. Utah was
given its proportion, a thousand dollars,
and nearly that amount was raised in small
donations from the school children. A lit-
tle over a hundred dollars is lacking to
make up the thousand and a unique pro-
gramme has been prepared by the commit-
tee who have had the work in charge,
which will be more than compensation for
the price of the tickets. It will not be a
social affair, but there will be music and
other pleasing features and a talk by Cap-
tain Ducat who was in the battle at Santi-
ago de Cuba.
	The meetings of the National Woman’s
Relief Society held this month have been
fraught with much interest and a general
good feeling has been manifested in the
charitable and philanthropic work of this
oldest organization; and the instruction
given by President Zina D. H. Young and
her counselors and assistants has been of a
nature calculated to bind together and
strengthen the work in the various locali-
ties, and to increase the interest of the Soci-
ety in the several lines of work necessary
to the accomplishment of the purposes de-
signed when the organization was effected.
To elevate and uplift the condition of those
who need assistance temporally or spiritual-
ly, to provide employment for the idle and
to secure the wheat that has been gathered
and held in trust as well as add to the quan-
ty now while bounteous harvests are being
garnered. These and many other matters
have been discussed by those residing in
this centre Stake of Zion and by those who
have come from a distance, until it would
seem these topics must be thoroughly
understood.


U. W. P. C.

	On Friday evening, Sep. 30, 1898, the
Press Club met as usual in the “Exponent”
office. Called to order by President Ro-
mania B. Pratt, Mrs. E. B. Wells made the
opening prayer. Roll call was answered
by sentiments from Shakespeare. Minutes
of previous meeting read and approved.
The business of incorporating the club was


brought up. A motion was made by Mrs.
E. B. Wells and seconded by Lydia D.
Alder that the Press Club be incorporated;
carried. Moved by Mrs. E. W. Hyde and
seconded by Ellis R. Shipp that the presi-
dent appoint a committee to attend to it;
carried. The president appointed Mrs. E.
B. Wells and Mrs. Margaret A. Caine.
	A letter was read by the corresponding
secretary, L. D. Alder, from the editors of
Woman’s Work thanking the club for their
splendid report, also asking for meritorious
papers written by club members; also read
a letter from the secretary of the State
Federation in regard to annual dues for
the coming year. It was moved by Mrs.
E. B. Wells and seconded by Ruth M. Fox
that the dues be paid; carried.
	What shall we do at our initial meeting?
was then discussed. Dr. Ellis R. Shipp
moved that we meet at Mrs. Bunnell’s home
125 First St.; carried. It was also decided
that the club have an open discussion on
the late queen of Denmark, the Dreyfus 
case and Hallowe’en, Dr. Ellis R. Shipp,
Helen V. Kimball and Ruth M. Fox lead-
ing in the respective topics.
	It was decided that we hold a business
meeting, Monday, October 10, at 7 o’clock
p. m., at the “Exponent” office.
	A most interesting and instructive paper
on the Empress Elizabeth of Austria was
read by Helen V. Kimball, and meeting
adjourned to meet at Mrs. Bunnell’s, Oct.
31. Dismissed by Lydia D. Alder.
			RUTH M. FOX, Act. Sec.


EMPRESS OF AUSTRIA.

ELIZABETH AMELIA EUGENIE, EMPRESS
OF AUSTRIA AND QUEEN OF HUNGARY.
	The people of Austria and Hungary were 
overwhelmed with sorrow and grief when
the dreadful tidings reached the palace that
their beloved empress had been brutally
assassinated at Geneva, Switzerland, on
September 10. The populace rushed into
the streets, anxiously inquiring for intelli-
gence, all hearts were filled with sadness
and a universal sympathy was expressed 
for the emperor, “upon whose head such
grief had fallen in the year of his Jubilee.”
	The empress was most beautiful, she has
been described as exquisitely lovely with
Narcissus fairness, great blue-black eyes,
teeth of pearly whiteness and magnificent
chestnut hair which fell to her knees; her 
ethereal form clouded in silk-embroidered,
diamond-studded gauze, with large emer-
alds glittering on her hair and bosom.
She was highly educated, conversed elo-
quently, played exquisitely on the zither
and organ, sang well and had great talent
for painting. Her vanity was in her hair
which she had brushed four hours every
day, during which time her reader read to 
her.
THE STORY OF HER BETROTHAL. 
	A marriage had been arranged for the
emperor with her eldest sister. The young 
monarch arrived at the castle of his future
father-in-law for the purpose of being
officially betrothed. He, however, fell
madly in love with Princess Elizabeth who
was yet in short dresses, and breaking off
his engagement with Princess Helen he
married the young sister.
	She disliked Vienna and the Viennese
and she passionately loved the Hungarian.
The Viennese had looked for a far grander


[Column 1]
alliance for their emperor, and considered
that the impoverished family of Duke Max-
imilian, who was not even a Royal High-
ness, was unworthy so great a distinction.
Consequently the young empress was dur-
ing the first ten or twelve years of her
marriage treated with coldness and disdain
by the public, by the arrogant Austrian
aristocracy and by the members of the im-
perial family and in particular by Arch-
duchess Sophia, the empress mother, who
being talented and strongminded exercised
an enormous influence over her son’s mind.
She became intensely jealous of her lovely
daughter-in-law and instigated many of
the stories circulated about her, that of the
staircase of the palace Schoenbrum being a
sample.
	Said a lady of her court: “So much of a
purely imaginative and mendacious nature
has been published during the past years
concerning the empress-queen of Austria-
Hungary by persons who have never had
an opportunity of obtaining authentic in-
formation about her, that it may possibly
interest readers to learn something of her
majesty’s character and mode of life from
one who has enjoyed the privilege of form-
ing one of her immediate circle.
	Elizabeth’s intense horror of publicity,
her love of seclusion and her exquisitely
beautiful and romantic personality have
rendered her the victim of misrepresenta-
tion that is ludicrous and frequently cruel.
Elizabeth, although regarded as an interest-
ing figure, is not one that is popular in the
general sense of the word, hence many of
these elaborately spun tissues of falsehood
have been of an inconsiderate and painful
character. One of the most unfounded of
these statements is the one to the effect that
her majesty has succumbed to the heredi-
tary curse of the house of Bavaria. She is
not demented, nor has she ever been so,
even momentarily. Broken-hearted by
sorrows, which were due to no fault of hers.
Yes; but not even a prey to melancholia so
graphically described by imaginative news-
paper writers.
	Her failure to acquire popularity was
due to her loftiness and great nobility of
character. Being remarkably gifted by
nature and education she was quick to per-
ceive the foibles and weaknesses of those
with whom she was brought into contact;
too honest to pretend with regard to the
same any blindness she did not feel, her
delicacy and purity of mind rendered her
intolerant with regard to the delinquencies
and indiscretions of a mundane’s life. She
condemned openly, and a mere look of her
glorious eyes was sufficient to convey a re-
proach to those whose consciences reproved
them.
	Her taste was refined, fastidious and
satircal. Love as ordinarily understood
was displeasing to her, and no man has
been able to obtain any influence over her;
when they attempted to change from
courtiers to open admirers they found an
impassable barrier between them in her
chilliness of manner. She has been sur-
rounded by all the powers of passion but
has never granted them more than a cool
smile of compassionate pity, those who
call her heartless prefering animals to
human beings, little know the true origin
of her reticence and coldness. She was
noble, silent, brave, resolute, generous and
absolutely truthful in all things.
	Francis Joseph has good looks, supreme
elegance, chivalry and a marvelous charm

[Column 2]
of manner. He is a soldier, a keen hunter,
a thinker, scholar, a satirist and is kind-
hearted. Although he has been drawn into
many so called affaires-de-coeur, he has
never loved but one woman, and that wo-
man is his wife. Not one tithe of the ad-
ventures ascribed to the Austrian monarch
are true. Women of all ranks admire him
and he never was able to withstand the
charm of this delightful homage; his soft-
heartedness in regard to women rendered
him cruel to the one he really loved. Often
at first the empress was angry, too proud to
stoop to jealousy. She bitterly suffered
when neglected, but found consolation in
religion, kind and warmhearted she helped
the unfortunate and consoled them as did
her namesake, Elizabeth of Hungary, cen-
turies ago. In her visits to the slums of
her empire numerous families were redeem-
ed from misery and suffering, many foul
places were purged to moral and physical
cleanliness and many young people male
and female were saved from crime and
shame.
	The empress was an enthusiastic horse-
woman and she smoked cigarettes and
strong cigars, which she said soothed her
nerves. It is a pity she could not have en-
joyed the splendor of her surroundings.
	Hapsburg, or Habsburg (named from the
old castle of Habsburg in Aargan, Swtzer-
land older name, Hawks Castle,) an ancient
and illustrious family to which belongs the
reigning sovereign of Austria. The castle,
which was built by Werner, Bishop of
Strassburg, 1001—29, is now in ruins, only
the walls of the tower remaining. The
founder of the house, however, was Albert,
who is mentioned as the Count of Hapsburg
in 1153. Under him and his son Rudolf I.
the family became the most powerful in
southern Germany and gained control of a
considerable part of Switzerland.
	The first German emperor of this family
was Rudolf I., who founded the Austrian
house which from 1438 to 1806 held the
German imperial crown, and since that
time has held that of Austria. In Spain,
Burgundy, Tuscany and Modena Haps-
burg monarchs, but of the Spanish line,
have also borne sway. The Spanish line
sprung from Charles V., whose father was
a son of Maximilian I., and whose mother
was a daughter of Ferdinand and Isabella.
When he abdicated in 1555 he left his Aus
trian possessions and the imperial crown of
Germany to Ferdinand I., Spain and the
Netherlands to his son, Philip II. With
Charles II. the Spanish line of the house of
Hapsburg became extinct 1700, and the
Spanish war of succession began, which left
Spain to the house of Bourbon.
	In 1881 it was proposed to purchase the
Castle of Hapsburg and present it to the
crown prince of Austria, but the people of
Aargan opposed it so violently the project
was abandoned. Imagine what it is to be
there where their wealth has been accumu-
lating for ages. Vienna, the imperial capi-
tal of Austria-Hungary, on the right bank
of the Danube, where occurs the only break
in the great chain of the Alps and Carpath-
an mountains which divide the northwest-
ern from the southwestern part of Central
Europe. Hence it was chosen by the
Romans as a place worthy of settlement
and fortification against the tribes north of
the Danube. During the middle ages it be-
came a great centre of trade between the
north, south, east and west, and thus ac-
quired a cosmopolitan character which

[Column 3]
renders it one of the most interesting and,-
beautiful cities of Europe. The city lies at
the base of the double-peaked Thahlenberg
(Bald mountain) on which is seen the border
of the Viennese woods, whose beauty,
render the environs of the city most attrac-
tive. The public and private buildings in-
clude some of the finest of modern architec-
ture. The Court theatre is of white marble 
and its sculptures, in portraits and allegorical
figures, tell the story of the world’s drama.
Two imperial museum buildings of great
extent face each other across a square, in
the center of which stands Conova’s great
monument of Maria Theresa, surrounded
by flowers and shrubbery amid which play
the waters of four exquisite marble foun-
tains.
	In the king’s palace the grand stairway
and entrance hall are most beautiful. As
you enter the palace there is a grotto of
shells with fountain. In the Crown room
are portraits in court costume of the present
emperor and empress. The palaces are so
beautiful one leaves them with regret.
Now that the empress rests in the solemn,
silent vaults of the Capuchin church where 
so many of the Hapsburgs are resting in
silver and in bronze coffins on the stone
floor beneath the arches, her many virtues
will become prominent.
				HELEN V. KIM
Read at the U. W. P. C.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


THE MARTHAS AND MARYS.

	In Luke chapter 10, verses 41-42, we
find the words, “Martha, Martha, thou art
careful and troubled about many things,
but Mary hath chosen the better part which
shall not be taken from her.”
	Now God forbid that I should be irrever-
ent or question the sayings of the Master, 
but it seems to me He was rather hard on
Martha on that occasion. I can only think
that the whole is not told and that there is 
more than we see in the Savior’s words.
	Martha was cumbered with much serv-
ing we are told, and it seems as if Mary
instead of lending a helping hand was sit-
ting at Jesus’ feet drinking in His wonder- 
ful words; perhaps he was expounding to
her the principles of the Gospel not perfect-
ly understood by her, and she in her rapt
attention forgot her sister laborers. But
Martha was working for Him, she was pre- 
paring the feast at which He was to join, 
but why was she less honored in her duties
than her sister? Doubtless she would
have liked to hear His wonderful words too,
but He might have had to depart or retire
supperless had she done so. I have often
thought of these words and wondered at
them
	There are many toiling Marthas today
who would gladly take the place of the
Marys, but by reason of their multifarious
duties have not the time.
	I picture before me a good and worthy
sister spending her life and her means in
every good act that comes in her way,
in fact giving her time and substance in the
service of the Master, her name honored,
her very presence felt and a murmur of
blessing following her on every side. I see
another sister whose aspirations are as high, 
whose love of the Master is as great and
who would gladly devote her life to His
ministry but who is poor and has to toil for
her daily bread, and has but little time to
devote to the better part. Her toils are


unrecognized, there is no murmur of ap-
plause, no one rises to call her blessed.
In the hereafter when the good Lord
judges all flesh, can it be possible that she
will have less glory than her more favored
sister?
	I see a patient, untiring mother with only
small means toiling from morning to night
for her husband and children with little or
no encouragement, (by the way, husbands
might often lift a great weight of care by
saying a cheering word of praise, they do
not think, how dear such trifles are to the
weary and careworn wife,) but still she
toils on. There are thousands of Marthas
bearing many heartaches that are not able
to take the place of the Marys, and whose
inmost soul cries out, “Bless me also, even
me O Lord!”
	The Master knew Martha well, for we
are told “He loved Martha and her sister,”
so she must have been thoroughly under-
stood by Him. Then why the rebuke?
Surely, surely there is a crown of glory for
the Marthas also!
	I shall be glad if any sister or brother
will throw a little light on the Savior’s
words, which I acknowledge I am unable
to understand.
					DILBHUR.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]
R. S. REPORTS.

ONEIDA STAKE
	Minutes of the Oneida Stake Relief Soci-
ety quarterly conference held in the Frank-
lin meeting house, Sep. 13, 1898.
	Morning Session: On the stand were
Stake President Elizabeth Fox, Counselor
Mary Hawks and Sister Elizabeth J. Stev-
enson of Salt Lake City. Meeting opened
by singing “Guide us O, Thou Great Jeho-
vah” Prayer by Sister Phebe McNeil.
singing “We thank Thee O, God for a
prophet.” The minutes of the previous
conference were read and approved.
	President Fox was pleased to see so
many present, as the Stake had been divid-
ed recently there would not be so many
wards represented as heretofore, there being
but twelve wards left in the Stake instead
of twenty-seven.
	President Phebe McNeil reported the
Fairview ward; Julia Ropen, Preston; L.
B. Benson, Whitney; Annie Challis, Frank-
lin. All are in a flourishing condition both
spiritually and financially.
	Counselor Mary Hawks said: “It takes
energy to push any great cause along. We
have cause to be proud of the noble women
at the head of these organizations. There
are also women of the world doing noble
deeds. We should watch our youth, many
allurements surround them, infidelity
abounds on the earth.”
	Sister E. J. Stevenson felt pleased to be
present. Said we had already heard many
truths, considered it a privilege in hearing
the minutes of the first organization of the
Relief Society of the Church. It is quite
proper for the young ladies to join these
associations We should teach our
daughters to be good housewives, indus-
trious workers and not mortgage our homes
for the vanities of the world. We shall be
tried all we can stand.
	Singing “O, Jesus the Giver.” Bene-
diction by Counselor Mary Hawks.
	Afternoon Session: Singing “Come O,
Thou king of Kings.” Prayer by James
Challis. Singing “Prayer is the soul’s sin-

[Column 2]
cere desire.” Dayton and St. Joseph wards
reported.
	President Fox was pleased with the ex-
cellent reports. “Our Stake is so small now
we will be able to visit every ward.” Spoke
of their travels through the Stake before its
division, considered it important to gather
in the grain in the season thereof. The
Lord has blest us with bounteous harvests,
we should make every effort to store our
grain.
	Sister Stevenson touched upon the sub-
ject of storing grain; said in the midst of
counsel there is safety. President Wood-
ruff has said the vials of wrath were being
poured out. We must take care of our
health and attend our meetings. Our sis-
ters are being called on missions, great re-
sponsibilities will rest upon our young
people. Advised the sisters to take the
“Exponent.”
	Statistical and financial report read and
accepted. Mary Hawks presented the
general board of Relief Society officers.
All were sustained, and also the officers of
this Stake.
	Bishop L. L. Hatch heartily endorsed
all that had been said. President Fox and
Counselor Mary Hawks encouraged all to
love one another and be united, unity is
strength.
	Conference adjourned for three months.
Singing “Come listen to a prophet’s voice.”
Benediction by James Herd.
				ANNIE HATCH, Sec.
SUMMIT STAKE.
	Minutes of semi-annual conference of
Summit Stake Relief Society held at Hen-
nefer, Tuesday, Sep. 6, President Mary J.
Atwood presiding. Meeting commenced at
10:20 by singing “Prayer is the soul’s sin-
cere desire.” Prayer by Brother Pascot.
Singing “Come listen to a prophet’s voice.”
	On the stand were Counselors Ann Cluff
and Ruth M. Pack, Dr. E. S. Barney of
Salt Lake City, and Stake President W. W.
Cluff. The minutes of the previous con-
ference were read and accepted. Stake re-
port read. Verbal reports were given by
President Ann Harder, Kamas; President
Elizabeth Lusty, Coalville; President
Louisa Wickle, Echo; Counselor Sarah E.
Sargent, Hoytsville; Counselor Hannah
Richen, Hennefer.
	Counselor Ruth M. Pack then addressed
the sisters, advising them to build good
granaries and lay up wheat. Said: “We
have an abundant harvest this year and the
grain is good, work in the time thereof then
your duties will be easy. Get the spirit of
God, have your lamps trimmed and burn-
ing, that you may be prepared for whatever
is before you. It is a grand thing to be a
Latter day Saint. We have to do our
work in this dispensation to prepare for the
future, ask the Lord for His holy spirit to
give you strength to perform every duty.”
	President Atwood introduced Dr. E. S.
Barney who spoke upon a variety of sub-
jects and said: “My only desire is to do
good to others. If you wish to be edified
you must give your strict attention and be
united, that you may draw out the speaker
according to your desires. I am pleased to
meet with you and with one of your officers,
Sister Ruth Pack, whom I was acquainted
with over fifty years ago in Nauvoo and
crossing the plains. You have good com-
petent officers in your Stake organization,
listen to their counsels. Lay up all the
wheat you can, it will keep if well protect-

[Column 3]
ed. I had some bread made from flour that
I had kept fourteen years, it was sweet and
good. I sealed it up in tin cans and it kept
good. When you talk or read to a congre-
gation, or when you pray, don’t hide your
face behind a book or down in your hands,
but lift up your head so that your voice
may go out clear and distinct to the hear-
ers, that they may all say amen. May the
Lord help us to understand our positions is
my desire in the name of Jesus Christ.
	The morning session closed by singing.
Benediction by President W. W. Cluff.
	Afternoon session called to order at 2
o’clock by President Atwood. After the
opening exercise roll was called and six
branches represented. Counselor Ann Cluff
then presented the officers of the Central
Board who were sustained unanimously.
The Summit Stake Relief Society officers
were presented and sustained; the ward
presidents with their counselors as they
now stand were presented and sustained
unanimously.
	Counselor Ann Cluff said: “We all know
this society cannot be carried on without
means, we should have charity not only
with our means, but with one another.
Teachers should enter into their duties
with all their heart and soul, be determined
to do right and all the good possible; re-
member that charity covers a multitude of
sins. I ask the Lord to help us all.”
	President Atwood said: “I am very well,
pleased with our conference, I thought as
the presidents were reporting and they said
there was no poor to need help, what a
blessed people. There are a few that are
sick and when you go to visit them go
clothed with the spirit of your calling and
they will be pleased and comforted with
your visit. Give what you have to give
and ask God to bless the suffering. Your
offers are ‘like bread cast upon the waters,
after many days they will return,’ in bless-
ings upon your heads. We should be bet-
ter acquainted with each other and be
thankful for our homes and surroundings.”
	Sister Barney spoke for some time, touch-
ing upon her medical studies, and address-
ing the teachers she said: “Don’t ever say
you are begging. You are soliciting means
to carry out one of God’s requirements to
assist the poor and needy. What have we
that is our own? Not a thing, all belongs
to the Lord, you are only stewards and
should be liberal. If we were living in the
united order this would all be done away
with, for we would all be equal. Tithes
and offerings bring forth the blessings of
heaven.”
	President W. W. Cluff announced the
time of our beloved President Wilford
Woodruff’s funeral and made a few re-
marks, saying if the sisters are not proud of
themselves the brethren are proud of them.
“Their reports are excellent and you should
be proud of your perfect organization in the
Relief Society. I always admired Sister
Barney’s energy and push. I have been
acquainted with her a long time and al-
ways found her full of energy. The teach-
ers who feel like they are begging don’t
have the spirit of their mission upon them.
You go forth to solicit means to carry on
that part of the work of the Lord, and you 
should all be earnest in your labors. This
conference now adjourns to meet in Coal-
ville the first Tuesday in May, 1899.”
	Singing “Hail Emanuel.” Benediction
by Edmond Eldredge. 
	MARY L. WOOLSTENHULME, Sec.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


IN MEMORIAM.

LYDIA EVELINE CARTER HALES. 
	Died at Bountiful, Utah, August 18, 1898, of
dysentery and old age, Mrs. Lydia Eveline Carter
Hales, wife of Stephen Ha[-]es; she has been a
widow for seventeen yea[-]s. Her husband died
in Salt Lake City, Utah, in 1861. Mrs. Lydia
Eveline Carter Hales was a native of Vermont.
When a child of ten years, at her father’s house
in the town of Benson, Rutland Co., Vermont,
Parley P. Pratt came into the town preaching the
doctrine of Christ. At that time her father,
Simeon Carter, was a Methodist preacher and
could quote the Bible with great facility. Elder
Pratt was not long in convincing Mr. Carter that
the Church of Jesus Christ was the one he was
looking for.
	At the time the Saints were gathering at Nau-
voo Mr. Carter with his wife, one son and Lydia
Eveline moved to Nauvoo. Lydia grew to wo-
manhood and was married to Stephen Hales Oct-
ober 16, 1832, by P. G. Sessions. They built
themselves a comfortable home but did not enjoy
it long, as a mob drove the Saints out from Nau-
voo. They then started for Utah; got as far as
Iowa and on account of sickness did not leave
for two years, arriving in Ut[-]h in the fall of 1851.
They stayed in Salt Lake City until 1868 when
they moved to Bountiful, where Mrs. Hales has
lived up to the time of her death. Mrs. Hales
has lived a quiet life and leaves numerous rela-
tives and friends to mourn her loss. She died a
faithful member of the Church.
	Funeral services were held in the South Boun-
tiful meeting house on Sunday afternoon August
21, at 2 o’clock.
							[COM.]
GONE TO HER REST.
	In the recent death of our aged relative and
friend, Sister Phebe Beable Rodeback, who de-
parted this life Sep. 17, at 11 p. m., t[-]is world lost
one of its [-]oblest souls and heaven gained as pure
and holy a spirit as ever entered its portals.
	Being an early pioneer she witnessed all the
trying scenes and annoying times which harassed
the Saints during the first settlement of our now
beautiful State, and with them she bore those
trials with patience and an unflinching heart.
Ever quiet, kind and loving, sh[-] was loved a[-]d
esteemed by all who knew her. She was so un-
selfish she thought always of others before her-
self, and so long as she was able her gentle, will-
ing hands were ever ready to aid others in their
hour of need and distress.
	Her mind, which for her advanced years, was
unusually bri[-]ht, and until the day of her death
she understood ever thing that was happening
around her, and talked intelligently on all the
leading events and topics of the times.
	Sister Rodeback and her husband were friends
of t[-]e Prophet Joseph Smith and often enter-
tained him at their home in Nauvoo They were
also very dear friends of Bishop Edward Hunter
and before their marriage in Pennsylvania we[-]e
both members of that good man’s household.
	Sister Rodeback passed from this earth at the
home of her daughter. Mrs. Rebecca Cook of
Cedar valley, Utah, to join her loved ones on the
golden shore, and to meet the God whom she
had loved and served so long and faithfully. She
was the widow of the late James Rodeback and
wa born in Chester Co., Pa., June 26, 1811. In
1839 she became a member of the Church of
Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints, and in 1840 or
41 removed to Nauvoo where she resided during
the troublous times there. With her husband
and six chi[-]dren she came to Utah in 1852, being
a memb[-]r of the late Uriah Curtis’ company
	Si[-]ter Rodeback remained a faithtul Latter-
day Saint until the end and has surely received
the promised great reward in heaven.
								L. E. C.


DEDICATION OF A GRANARY AT
MILL CREEK.

	The Relief Society of Mill Creek ward,
Salt Lake Co, have just completed a sub-
stantial wheat bin with a capacity of fifteen
hundred bushels, at a cost of about two
hundred dollars. The bin is situated at
Bishop Jas. C. Hamilton’s.
	The members of the Relief Society, their
husbands, the officers of the Young Men’s
and Young Ladies associations, also Patri-


arch Archibald Hill and Bishop John R.
Winder and wife were invited to assemble
and dedicate the same, Tuesday, September
20. Bishop Hamilton placed his fine resi-
dence and grounds at the disposal of the
Society and the following program was
rendered: The choir under the leadership
of Brother Richard M. Horne sang “Praise
Ye the Lord.” Prayer was offered by
Patriarch Hill. Another song was render-
ed by the choir and an essay on wheat was
read by Secretary Frances Hanson. Brother
Edward White sang a song, and those pres-
ent then repaired to the bin and as many as
so wished inspected, the same. Bishop
John R. Winder, kneeling on the steps,
offered the dedicatory prayer. It was a
very imposing ceremony, and all seemed to
feel that it was a step in the right direction.
The audience returned to the lawn and the
choir sang “We Have Thought of Thy Kind-
ness.” Counselor Mary B. Hamilton recit-
ed a selection, “The Curse of the Dram
Shop.” Sister Sarah F. Boden sang, “Do
They Miss Me at Home?” Counselor James
R. Miller, Brother R. M. Horne, Bishop J.
R. Winder and Bishop J. C. Hamilton each
spoke in reference to the storing of grain by
the Relief Society. President Elizabeth
Hill in a few well chosen words thanked all
those who had assisted in making the under-
taking a success.
	One hundred and thirty then sat down at
one long table arranged across the lawn,
which was loaded with the bounties of the
earth. The young ladies who acted as
waiters, also the committee on arrangements
deserve special mention. After supper there
was music and singing. It was an occasion
long to be remembered, and the unity which
prevailed caused all to return home well
satisfied with the day’s proceedings.
FRANCES H. HANSON, Sec.


[Column 1]
STAND BY AMERICAN PRINCIPLES.

Indianapolis, Ind., Sep. 15, 1898.
	To me it is a bitter disappointment that
our great Republic had not gotten on far
enough to do righteousness and to compel
another nation to do righteousness without
resorting to bloodshed. To my mind there
is a contradiction in the words "Civilized
war," and a more emphatic contradiction
in the words "Christian war."
	It seemed six months ago that our coun-
try would rally all the moral forces latent
in the people, by a diplomatic use of those
forces to rescue Cuba from tyranny.
Whether it might have done so we can
never know; that it did not is certain. Those
of us who deplored the war, as well as those
who invoked it, must feel a patriotic pride
in the courage and gallantry that our men
have manifested in every encounter, wheth-
er by land or sea. We must feel an equal
pride in the heroism with which they have
borne hardships not necessary even in war-
fare.
	But the pride we feel in that physical
heroism, which could not have been mani-
fested without an undercurrent of moral
heroism, must not blind us to the dangers
that assail our country in its hour of
triumph. If ever a nation accompanied a
declaration of war with an avowal of abso-
lute unselfishness, such declaration was
made by the United States through its
Senate in the resolution which disavowed
any intention of territorial aggression. Now
the war being ended, temporarily at least,

[Column 2]
one can hardly pick up a newspaper of
any party, or even a religious newspaper of
any sect without encountering such phrases
as "Our new territory," "The conquered
provinces," "The rightful spoils of the con-
querer." "Our conquests," "Our new
colonial possessions," etc- What do these
phrases mean? Do they mean that as a
nation we have forgotten our disavowal of
any desire to increase our territory by the
war with Spain? Does it mean that we
have really resumed the mediæval standard,
and believe in military conquests? Does it
mean that the ideal of Cæsar and the ambi-
tion of Napoleon have displaced the ideas of
Washington and his associates?
	How can we talk of "Our new posses-
sions?" Do we own the Ladrones by con-
quest? Have we a right to replace in
Cuba the domination of the Spaniard by
the domination of the United States?
What is the meaning of this discussion
of whether we shall annex the Philippines
as a group, or be satisfied with taking a few
of the islands?
	These discussions, and the constant re-
currence of phrases like those quoted above
seem to indicate that we are maddened by
the taste of blood, and that the influence of
the war, or of our success in it, has been to
blind us to the principles which we have
avowed, the principles indeed upon which
our Government rests.
	For myself I have never been abroad
without feeling, in the presence of the uni-
formed soldiery on every hand, a reminder
of the blessed condition of my own land. I
have been more proud and more grateful
for our having no standing army and no
navy than for any possessions that my
country could boast, I have heard hun-
dreds of American citizens express the same
sentiments, and have read columns of cor-
respondence and editorial and other news-
paper writing, the sole object of which was
to congratulate the citizens of the United
States on being free from militarism. Yet
now we are as eager to have the largest
navy in the world, to increase our standing
army and to become "a military power" as
Germany itself. Do we believe that
"government derives its just power from
the consent of the governed?" Can we
pretend that we believe this, and then set
up a "military government" in what we
call our "conquered provinces?"
	It seems that one encounters ridicule by
even asking these questions. We are told
that the "Idealism which did very well for
a young nation, for a youthful people de-
veloping under pioneer conditions, is not
for seventy millions with a navy strong
enough to be a balance of power in the
Eastern question." This scornful rejection
of our ideals, one encounters in nine out of
every ten newspaper paragraphs that she
sees, and in nine but of every ten public
speeches that she hears.
	Are we, then, to confess that the ideals,
which for more than a century we have
boasted were destined to secure the ultimate
union of all the peoples of the world in a
federation of republics, are fit only for peo-
ple just emerging fron colonial subjection,
and not at all for people developed into im-
perial strength? Can only the puny and
the few rejoice in ideals? And are these
most precious of all possessions to be sur-
rendered by the numerous and the strong?
	The immediate first duty of every good
American, of every good citizen of a repub-
lic, of every believer in the doctrine of self-
government, of every man or woman who


has ever boasted that our country was a
refuge of the oppressed, and that our
Government was framed upon principles to
which the world in the evolutionary pro-
cess would come, is to expunge the obnox-
ious monarchial, anti-democratic, anti-
Christian phrases that are polluting our
speech. “Remember the Maine,” that
direct appeal to the sentiment of revenge,
“The rights of the conquering nations,”
that direct confession of allegiance to
Ceasarism—let these phrases and all their
numerous synonyms be compelled to go.
MAY WRIGHT SEWALL.
—Ex.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


LADIES SEMI-MONTHLY MEETING.
Minutes of Ladies Semi-monthly meeting
held in the 14th ward Assembly hall, Aug-
ust 27, 1898, Counselor E. J. Stevenson
presiding. Opened by singing "For the
strength of the hills we bless thee.” Pray-
er by Counselor E. L. Webb. Continued
by singing ‘‘Welcome best of all good
meetings.” Sister Stevenson stated that
President Horne would not be able to meet
with us today and asked the sisters to ex-
ercise faith that Sister Horne might be
strengthened, and her heart made to re-
joice. Minutes read and approved.
Counselor E. J. Stevenson gave valuable
instruction and spoke of the accidental
death of Sister Horne’s grandson. Our
sympathies go out to those who are afflicted
and bereaved.
Sister E. Barrow thought we should live
by every word that proceedeth from the word
of God, and made other good remarks.
Sister Julia C. Howe said: “The love of
the Lord should be written in our hearts.
I think we have been negligent in not
teaching our children the ten command-
ments. Do we instruct our children what
these sins are? Parents should teach their
children all the virtues and then send them
to the Primary for further instruction. We
must teach our children by the fireside.
Sisters pray for those who are bereft in the
death of young Brother Horne, it is a
shock to our dear Sister Horne. May the
love of the Lord be imprinted on the hearts
of our children, and if we do not teach
them aright we will be held accountable.”
Sister M. W. Wilcox said: “May we be
enabled to comprehend the dealings of God
with us, honor the priesthood, exercise
faith in the Gospel, pray for our children
when they are going away from us, and I
believe God will respect us for it.”
Sister Barnes, Sister Martin and Sister
Rawlings each bore a faithful testimony.
The congregation sang “Lord Thou wilt
hear me when I pray.”
Sister Jane James made a few remarks.
Sister E. Webb spoke of the evil of allow-
ing our children to be out at night.
Sister Stevenson made a few closing re-
marks and meeting adjourned for two
weeks. Singing Doxology. Benediction
by Sister Margaret P. Young. 
LYDIA D. ALDER, Sec. pro tem.


NOTES AND NEWS.

Miss Annie Wheeler, daughter of Gener-
al Wheeler, who had been nursing the sick
at Santiago, and has now come up to
Montauk, has become especially endeared
to the men for her adaptability under all
circumstances. On the voyage from Santi-


ago to Montauk, Miss Wheeler several
times acted as chaplain in reading the pray-
ers for the dead as the bodies were con-
signed to the watery grave.

Mrs. Ellen Louise Demorest, founder of
Demorest’s Monthly Magazine, died recent-
ly in New York City, after a long illness.
Mrs. Demorest had been actively engaged
for many years in philanthropic and temper-
ance work and in publishing the magazine
and other literary and fashionable periodi-
cals. She was born in Saratoga in 1824,
was the president of the Woman’s Christian
Temperance Union of New York City; vice-
president and trustee of the Woman’s Tem-
ple of Chicago; one of the charter members
and vice-president of Sor[-]sis; and a mem-
ber of many other societies. Mrs. Demo-
rest leaves four children—Mrs. Vienna
Demorest Gano, Henry C. Demorest, Wil-
liam C. Demorest and Mrs. Evelyn Demo-
rest Rea. 
Clara Barton will be ready to meet Sur-
geon-General Sternberg or anybody else
who wishes to lay responsibility for suffer-
ing on the Red Cross. It is not likely that
that official will be eager to hear the truth
all told by that straightforward lady. The
Red Cross took the lion’s share of the re-
sponsibility of relieving suffering so long
that Surgeon-General Sternberg’s announce-
ment is ridiculous. He says that inasmuch
as the Red Cross has had entrance to the
military camps since June 9 they must share
the responsibility for any suffering with the
War Department. The Red Cross isn’t
afraid but who is going to “investigate”
the merciful acts of a volunteer organiza-
tion managed by a disfranchised female
citizen?—Boston Transcript.
A small dish of powdered charcoal kep.
on one of the upper shelves of the refriger-
ator is an excellent thing to absorb odors.
It should be changed every few days.

SWIFT AS THE EAGLE 
Denver is one day nearer Chicago to-day than
it was yesterday. This is the actual practical
effect of the fast mail trains inaugurated by the
North-Western and Union Pacific.—Chicago
Times Herald, Feb. 8, 1898.
“The Chicago Special"
	Leaves Denver, 4:30 p m today.
	Arrives Omaha, 7:00 a m tomorrow.
	Arrives Chicago, 8:45 p m tomorrow.
	To the Chicago, Union Pacific and North-
western belongs the credit of inaugurating the
new fast train service between Denver and
Chicago.
□ This new train is a triumph of modern rail-
roading. It consists of six magnificent cars:
Buffet, smoking and library car, with barber
shop, etc. Two splendid palace sleeping cars.
Reclining chair car. Dining car, meals a la
carte. Baggage car. 

	This is the only train between Denver and
Chicago having the luxurious

Westbound, “The Colorado Special" leaves
Chicago 10:00 a m, arrives Omaha 11:55 p m, to-
day, and Denver l:30 p m, tomorrow.
