AUTUMN.

Dismal, doleful katydid
	Sad is thy song this autumn night,
It speaks of death to the budding rose,
	It tells of the swallow’s homeward flight—
Yes every bud will droop and die,
And every bird will southward fly.

The bees that in the forest rove,
	Have gathered in their winter’s store,
And soon will hide themselves away,
	Where we shall hear their songs no more—
Happy, busy little bee,
Sadly do we part with thee.

The meadow brook must speed away
	And join the river deep and wide,
The squirrels must cease their merry play,
	And in the giant oak tree hide—
Frisking squirrel and babbling brook,
We give to both a last, long look.

Waving trees with golden garb,
	Softly sigh of dreary days,
And beyond the hill crest high
	See the veil of gathering haze—
Hark! I hear a mournful bell,
	’Tis sweet summer’s funeral knell.

Summertime with beauty rare,
	Decked with flowers rich and gay,
Strewing sunshine everywhere,
	Why—Oh! why canst thou not stay?
An echo through the mountain rings,
And sorrowfully this answer brings:

What we prize so quickly fades, 
	All we love must someday die,
Even thou must leave us soon,
	And soar above yon azure sky,
There summertime with garlands fair
A crown will make which thou shalt wear.
			NINA WINSLOW ECKART.


[Column 1]
THE CASTLE MOUNTAINS.
	The day is a clear and a beautiful one,
just as heralded by the early dawn whose
rosy tints are painted on the sombre gray
that envelopes the earth like the folds of a
mantle. Soon we are bestirring ourselves
making preparations for a long drive over
to Huntington in Emery Co. Fresh
breezes come stealing down from the moun-
tains with exhilarating effect as we present-
ly start out on our journey. All are so
cheerful and companionable, earnestly en-
gaging in thoughtful conversation, some-
times bantering about the good roads which

[Column 2]
we momentarily expect to find, although
they seem to ever evade us.
	Up the mountain, through the cañons,
down into the valleys, always failing to
find the best road, but being hopeful we
freely converse, scarcely realizing how long
the way we have to go.
	Here we are on the buckboard slats so
well and aptly named, as we are thorough-
ly bumped in riding over the many undula-
tions still searching for the best roads and
which ever one we take we sigh that we
had not taken the other; but as no break-
downs are in evidence we boldly defy fate
and ride on to victory or death.
	These are the Castle mountains! this
beautiful chain now full in view challenges
our admiration, and one could well believe
that just beyond those towering peaks lies
the famous Robbers Roost, where the rob-
bers securely retire up a a steep precipice,
and at the opening on top one man can
guard the haunts invested by the law
breakers.
	Turning a mountain that rises suddenly
a seeming city rises full in view. The
mountains are appropriately named, for
they indeed resemble castles such as we
read of in the old Fuedal times, whose em-
bankments rise almost perpendicularly, and
we can fancy the moat must be there and
where, the drawbridge being raised, they
are almost inpregnable to all enemies.
Golden shadows of light reflect below them
showing of their grand designs to perfec-
tion, and the traveler imagines that many
magnificent cities lie just over there. None
of their inhabitants are visible, but then
they may have slept so long that they have
forgotten to wake. Ages of silence have
passed, that now is interrupted by the en-
gine bell and the rattling of wheels. In
meditation we gaze on these vast and
wonderful piles of rocks and their solemn
silence guards well their secrets of long ago.
Were they once cities with high domes
pointing heavenward? They appear so
real that one does not readily recall the
wandering thoughts that turning backward
scan the past whose history these mute
sentinels guard so well. Some are high
and rugged, others square with sudden
defiles, others look like turrets where the
bells were wont to wildly ring for joy and
gladness, or to toll for loss or pain in sad-
ness.
	Pretty Huntington so well laid out comes
into view as we descend the mountains.
And such a warm welcome we receive,
and such true hearts we meet, making our
sojourn both pleasant and profitable. But
all friends must part and so we leave these
pleasant associations and return to loving
welcomes in Price, a growing and prosper-
ous town where only a dozen years ago
scarcely a house stood, yet now the water
flows on either side of the streets and rows
of shade trees adorn them. How pleasant
the memory of friends we met here! On a
Sabbath morn when a holy hush falls on
the weary heart we leave for Castle Gate.
parting with one of our companions whom
we miss. There by kindest invitation we
are to spend this lovely day.

[Column 3]
	The iron monster comes screeching
along, waiting a moment while we enter
then away for only a short ride. Twenty
minutes at Helper. What a lovely spot!
After crossing the bleak and dreary summit
it seems like a veritable oasis in a desert,
yet amid the grandest scenery. Lawns
tastefully laid out as green as in the spring- 
time, houses freshly painted with electric
wires attached, a large hotel, everything
in a bustle, escaping steam from the iron
horse, loading and unloading freight, then
onward again to visit at Castle Gate. We
are met at the depot by the Bishop and one
of his counselors also the Bishop’s wife,
all bidding us welcome. A glad touch of
hands and we have been long acquainted.
Here the well known Castle Gate coal is
mined in large quanties by these sturdy
Welsh people who dwell in these clean
looking red houses with white painted
window and door frames, grouped here and
there in the little valley. Early and late
these miners toil taking out the treasures
from these everlasting hills, which have
been hidden from time immemorial.
	Meeting with the Sabbath School we are
charmed with the interest manifested, and
feel proud of the superintendent who so
pleasantly invites us to visit at his home.
Meeting in the afternoon, the Lord’s Sup-
per so reverently administered amid the
glorious strains of melody from the Welsh
choir. Even as the surrounding mountains
soar high in triumph then descend in hu-
mility, so sang this choir in this lovely spot
so far away it seems from the haunts of
men.
	In the evening how the lurid flames
burst out from the coke furnace, gilding the
nearest mountains with deepest crimson.
At their feet the Castle river booms over
hugh bolders of rock, dashing the spray on
its unceasing journey to the sea. The wind
rises and shrieking blows over the moun-
tains, carrying the furnace flames high in
the air. Truly the scene is magnificent,
but wild beyond compare and one could
fancy it a view of Hades. Still the wind
rises; the reflections of the roaring flames
seem to beat out fury against the rugged
mountains which seem to restrain their
blazing advances. The storm bursts; the
wind rushing and sobbing, then roaring as
though destruction were near. The rain
descends, the wind more sullenly blows,
and though the rain subsides the wind
holds high carnival through the night.
Once more we view the scene so grand, yet
breathing a sense of solemn awe.
	On the morrow good bye again and fare-
well to these good and noble friends, and
may we meet again. The rushing train
slows up, we step aboard, then hence to the
city by the Great Salt Lake.

	As we travel on o’er [-]ife’s rough journey,
		How oft we cross the street Good Bye! 
	At morn clasp hands whose touch is gladness,
		At eve to part with many a sigh.

	But this is life, Good Bye, Good Bye,
		Ye first we’ve met, e’er yet we part,
	To meet is joy, the parting’s pain,
		And presses heavy on the heart.


The time will come when no Good Byes,
	Will ever jar upon the ear;  
We’ll meet, we’ll meet with no farewells,
	Where never’s known the falling tear.
But on life’s way, steep mountains frowning,
	Bar the traveler from a world so fair;
Where o’er the summits bright, unchanging,
	Is the City Golden. May we meet up there.
				LYDIA D. ALDER.
October, 1898.


WOMANS EXPONENT


[Column 1]
REAPERS CLUB.
	Extracts from the minutes of the Reaper’s
Club meeting held on the 12th of Septem-
ber, 1898, at the “Woman’s Exponent”
office:
	An interesting paper on the “Ruins of
Central America” was read by Mrs. J.
Druce. She stated that in 1839 Mr. J. L.
Stephens was intrusted with a special mis-
sion to Central America by President Mar-
tin Van Buren. He visited the ruins in
that country, including Copan, Chiapas,
Yucatan and others, Mr. Stephens found
on the left bank of the Copan river in Hon-
duras, an ancient and mysterious city situ-
ated about three hundred miles from the
sea.
	A Spanish historian of Guatemala stated
that in 1530 the Cacique of Copan and
others were engaged in a revolt against
Spanish rule, and being determined to sub-
due them the Spaniards laid siege to the
city known as Copan, which at that time
was one of the largest and most popular in
the kingdom. After a desperate battle the
Cacique and his auxiliaries being unable to
resist their foe retreated and left Copan to
its fate. The place described by this
Spanish writer must have had reference to
some other city, as the massive stone
structures and columns showed no signs
of any such battle having occurred.
	Francisco Fuentes mentioned that in the
year 1700 the great Circus of Copan still re-
mained entire. “At a short distance from
this stood a wall with a gateway and pas-
sage leading to two fine pyramids from
which was suspended a hammock contain-
ing two human figures. It appeared to be
entirely of one stone and of enormous
weight, yet was put in motion by the
slightest movement of the hand.”
	From 1700 until 1836 no mention had
been made of these ruins. In the latter
year Colonel Galendo under a commission
from the government of Central America
visited Copan and other ruins of that coun-
try, but of his report very little is known.
	In 1840 Mr. J. L. Stephens explored
some of those places especially Copan and
in order to save trouble with the natives
he pruchased from them about 6,000 acres
of land on which the ruins were situated.
The remains of Copan City extended for
two miles along a river of the same name,
and comprised the walls of a temple 62
feet long.”
	Mr. Stephens said: “Cutting our way
through dense woods we came to a square
column fourteen feet high and three feet on
each side, sculptured in very bold relief
from the base to the top. On the front
was the figure of a man curiously and rich-
ly dressed, whose face was evidently a por-
trait. The sides were covered with hiero-
glyphics. Before it and about three feet
away was a large block of stone, sculptured
with figures and supposed to have been an
altar.”

[Column 2]
	The hieroglyphics, figures, etc., found
among these ruins were beautifully carved
and of the finest workmanship.” These
and other discoveries prove that the people
who once occupied this American Conti-
nent were a cultured and enlightened race.
“The whole extent occupied by these remains
of a once great civilization was covered
with trees. Two of them were gigantic
ciebas over twenty feet in circumference,
binding down the ruins and shading them
with their branches. Who were the build-
ers of this city? Historians say that
America was peopled by savages, but such
a class never reared these structures. The
fine carving on stone was the work of an
enlightened race who must have been edu-
cated in the arts and sciences of their day.
The Indians were asked who made these
monuments, etc., and their answer was,
‘Who knows?’ At one point on the south-
east corner of an enclosure is a massive
pyramidal structure 120 feet high.
	On the right are also remains of terraces,
pyramidal buildings and a gateway twenty
feet wide, the latter entering into a quad-
rangular area 250 feet square, two sides of
which are pyramids 120 feet high.
	At different points of this area are numer-
ous remains of sculpture. Lying on the
ground was a remarkable portrait, probably
of some king or chieftan. The expression
was noble. Near this stood a column alone
with its face to the east. It was 13 feet
high. 4 feet front 3 feet deep and sculp-
tured on all four sides. Originally it was
painted or enameled red, the color being
still visible.
	At a distance of perhaps 8 feet was another
large, elaborately sculptured stone with
rows of hieroglyphics on three of its sides. 
	Following the wall was still another of
the same size and similar in appearance.
	Turning north on the left was found a
high pyramidal structure. The range of
ruins continued on this side for about 400
feet, then turned at right angles to the left
and ran to the river, joining the other ex-
tremity of the wall at which the survey
commenced.
	There was another monument of differ-
ent shape, being larger at the top than be-
low, and the carvings on that was in low
relief. It alao had hieroglyphics.”
	Near the latter obelisk they found a very
remarkable altar, which stood on four globes
cut out of the same stone from which the
other portion of the structure was made,
the sculpture on this being in bas relief.
It was the only specimen of that kind of
carving found at Copan, all the others were
in bold relief. “This altar was six feet
square and 4 feet high, the top being di-
vided into 36 tablets inscribed with hiero-
glyphics, which probably recorded the his-
tory of great events in the lives of that
people. On each of the four sides of this
altar were carved four figures. The two
principal personages were on the west side
and facing each other. The serpent was
introduced in sculpture, reminding one of
the Egyptian methods of honoring their
heroes or kings. The head dresses were
curious and complicated, and the figures on
the altar all had breastplates.
	One of the two principal of these held in
his hand an object which seemed to be a
sceptre, and each of the others was hold-
ing something which might have been a
weapon of war, but if so, it was the only
representation of that kind of weapon found
in Copan; therefore, the supposition is that
they were a peaceful people.

[Column 3]
	The other courtyard was nearer the
river and probably may have been the
great circus mentioned by Fuentes, but in-
stead of being circular it was rectangular—
140 feet long and 90 feet broad, with steps
on all sides. It was more probably a por-
tion of the temple, and perhaps had been
the scene of imposing religious ceremonies.
There was found among Copan’s ruins a
small death’s head made from a fine green
stone (jadeite) and beautifully carved.
	How large stones were transported over
irregular surfaces for long distances, and
especially how one of them was set on a
mountain 2,000 feet high is a problem im-
possible for us to discover, or even, con-
jecture. At one point in a ravine leading
towards the river was a gigantic block
much larger than any seen in the city, and
was probably on its way there when the
labors of the workmen were suddenly ar-
rested.” Perhaps the history of the peo-
ple is engraven on their monuments etc.,
but who shall read them.
	These works were strange in design, ex-
cellent in sculpture, rich in ornament and
different from that of any people now known.
	Mr. Stephens made especial inquiry for
the stone hammock mentioned by Fuentes
but failed to find it, although it may still
be there broken and buried. A priest told
Mr. Stephens that he had seen it, and an
Indian also stated that it had been entire
two generations back.
	The existence of these ruins was first
made known to the world through the
writings of Mr. Stephens and the drawings
which accompanied them, by Mr. Cather-
wood. The latter gentleman was with Mr.
Stephens in Central America.
				J. A. DRUCE, Sec.


IN MEMORIAM.
 
		HENRIETTA STANDAGE.

	Died at Mesa City, Oct. 11, 1898, af er an illness
of some days, Henrietta Standage, wife of Henry
Standage one of the Mormon Battalion, and
daughter of Noah Rogers, one of the first mis-
sionaries to the Sandwich [-]sl[-]nds,and his wifeEda
Rogers. Deceased was born May 30, 1832, at
Shelbyville, Portage Co , Ohio. Born at so
early a period she passed through every principal
evolution of the Church  experience from the
day of her birth until the day of her death. She
came to Utah in 1850 and was married to Henry
Standage in 1851, with whom she shared in the
privations and toils of Utah’s early pioneers, and
to whom she bore a family of ten children. In the
year 1880 she emigrated from Utah to Arizona.
On her arrival in Mesa, Arizona, with her family
she joined the Relief Society in Alma ward, and
in 1883 she was voted in a first counselor in the
Relief [-]ociety of which she has been a faithful
member attendin every duty devolving upon her
and always on hand to help to relieve the sick or
those in distress; and as stated by her Bishop and
each who spoke at her funeral, Sister Standage
was a live member of the Church, was faithful to
every duty and calli g. From age, experience
and good works she has gone to her rest as a
veteran in the Church of God, leaving a veteran
husband with eight living children, forty-four
[-]randchildren and four great grandchildren to
bear her name in honor and blessing through all
generations of time.
	A long cortege of loving kindred and friends
followed her remains  to the grave to await a re-
union of loved ones in that sweet bye and bye.

	   "Dearest moth r thou hast left us,
			And thy loss we deeply feel,
	    But ’tis God that hast bereft us,
			He can all our sorrows heal.
	    Yet again we hope to meet thee,
			When the day of life is fled,
	    When in heaven with joy to greet thee,
			Where no farewell tear is shed.”

			     ANNA KLEINMAN, Pres.
				     SOPHRONIA A. STEWART, Sec.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


IS THE WAR OVER?

They say that peace has been proclaimed
	And the dreadful war is o’er,
And the chime of bells the glad news tells
	As it wafts from shore to shore.
The shouts of acclamation rise
	From every heart and tongue,
Of the deeds of valor of our men
	And the noble work they’ve done.

They have counted up the men that fell
	On each conflicting side;
And as results are brought to light
	They smile with National pride— 
As figures do not make mistakes
	But counteth all things plain, 
So we find but few of our boys in blue,
	Were numbered with the slain.

And from the facts as claimed above
	Expressions have been free, 
That from the first no land could claim, 
Such wondrous victory,
Success at[-]ended every move, 
And not by cha[-]ce they say,
But Providence did intervene
	And lead them on their way.

But bear with me and I will dare
	A protest to main[-]ain,
And think it far too soon to count
	The number of the slain,
As there are still, and yet may be
	More added to the dead,
Such is the news that greets our ears
	As the papers o’er are read. 
Oh! piteous sight on those cots laid low
	Those once brave, stalwart forms,
Who faced the dangers day and night
	Through scorching heat and storms.
Seventy hours ’tis said they fought, 
Almost unceasingly,
With purely true, ueselfish aim
	In the cause of liberty.

Oh! do not shout aloud too soon
	Nor count the victory won; 
Till each mother’s boy is in her arms
	That death don’t claim its own;
For there are weapons worse by far 
Than swords that hew men down,
And aggravate their sufferings more 
As fever, chills and wounds. 
LETITIA WILLIAMS.
October 14, 1898.


[Column 1]		
UTAH WOMAN'S PRESS CLUB.

	PRESIDENT'S FAREWELL ADDRESS.

	It seems only a short time ago that I had
the honor conferred upon me of being
elected president of the U. W. P. club
	In retrospective resume many excellent
articles of poetry and prose rise on my horo-
scope, shimmering with brilliant gems of
thought which have gladdened the hearts
and enriched the minds of our members.
	In November our hospitable member,
Mrs.Clarence M. Bradley, entertained us at
her pleasant home where we listened to
Thanksgiving stories of ye olden times and
a pretty original one by Mrs. E. B. Wells,
the evening winding up in a blaze of glory,
eating golden pumpkin pie and sipping the
nectar of the orient, delicious coffee.
	In December we wandered far in thought
journeying over to old, old China, taking a
bird’s eye view of these ancient and
unprogressive people with queues and queer
mode of life religious and moral; who bit-
terly resist any innovation of modern ways
and .ideas. But the fiat had gone forth and

[Column 2]
China too must cast her old traditions upon
the highway to be crushed under the wheels
of the 19th century car of progress filled
with new ideas. The isles of the sea can-
not escape the keen eye of adventure and
they too must come into broader walks of
intelligence. The interesting article on
Polynesia was a report of actual experiences
and eye witness of pictures of the isles of
the Pacific ocean as seen by the reader’s
husband in his great trip around the world.
This and other papers was illustrated by
specimens and trophies from the island and
the far off Indies gathered while in those
lands.
	In January in the very pleasant room in
which we are now assembled a motion was
made and carried that the club join the
General Federation of Clubs. We then
took a trip in our mind's eye to Germany
and looked over her picturesqe country and
peeped into the lives of many of the “Nota-
ble men and women” of that land.
	That night a deep laid and mysterious
conspiracy was completed which ended in
something of a tempest, but loving friends
tried and true held steadfast on their way
and on Feb. 26 a large company assembled
at Dr. E. R. Shipp’s residence where an
ovation was given Mrs. E. B. Wells as a
surprise. It was in honor of the coming
out of her sweet book of poems styled
“Musings and Memories.”
	The crowning expression of our appre-
ciation of her book was the presentation of
our book to her. In this book each leaf re-
flected personally the love and good wishes
of a friend, in original or selected prose or
poetry, with somewhere on the leaf a quo-
tation from her book and also adorned with
beautiful hand painted flowers, expressing
the feelings and tastes of the giver of the
leaf. There are nearly one hundred leaves
in the book, making an unique and costly 
memento of the birth of her brain-child and
also of the seventieth year of her life.
	In March the club had the rare honor to
entertain the world renowned author, F.
Marion Crawford, at a reception they gave
March 22, at the residence of Hon. Spencer
Clawson, the Bee Hive House, Miss Claire,
the young and pretty hostess, making
everything pleasant and beautiful for the
occasion.
	 On March 6 the club met instead of Feb-
ruary 28, when we enjoyed a word pano-
rama of France and were highly entertained 
with short histories of some of her famous
men and women.
	March 31 our program consisted of an in-
teresting paper on Portugal and an eloquent
plea for “Cuba Libre.”
	In April, the month of sunshine and
showers “so full of sweet variety,” the life
and arduous labors of our spirit friend Miss
Frances E. Willard was ably presented and
discussed or new light added to as occurred
to the minds of the members. Also an im-
pressive paper recounting the life of the
brave Zola and a scathing, prophetic poem
styled Hispania.
	May 31 we heard of the Royal Family 
of Spain. Spain is fast going into oblivion
and we cannot weep, for her loss of power
is the gain of every released subject. With-
out a pang we say “au revoir” Spain.
	The month of June, beautiful month of
roses and every sweet perfume, seems as
barren of club history as the Desert of 
Sahara so far as I can find, and come to re-
member we had no meeting that month
but adjourned and went to the concert in
the Tabernacle. But a reception commit

[Column 3]
tee met the ladies from the north who were
on their way to Denver to the General
Federation and gave them a trolley ride and
otherwise entertained them while birds of
passage.
	In July your president received a letter to
the club from Second Vice-President Helen
V. Kimball dated July 8, Alvalon, Catalina
Island, California, Hotel Metropole. How
delightful it would be if the club could
take an outing to Catalina Island and look
through the glass bottomed boat at the
beauties and wonders of the sea.
	On July 30, the regular club day,we had
a most instructive paper on the origin,
work and purpose of the Red Cross Society,
and one on Modern Warfare.
	August 8 was made a most auspicious
and long to be remembered day to your presi-
dent by the members tendering her a most
happy surprise. The most kindly solici-
tude was expressed for her welfare as she
silently wends her way down the shady
side of life, having just left behind her the
59th milestone. A beautiful medalion 
picture of Queen Louise among her people,
together with many most beautiful flowers
were presented to her, which strongly im-
pressed her that life is still sweet amidst the
love of friends and lovely flowers, and that
there is much time left to smooth the pil-
low of sorrow and pain and to comfort the
hearts of the unfortunate.
	No club meeting was held this month an
adjournment having been decided upon un-
til September 30.
	The club members had decided to take an
outing to Saltair in August, but the gods of
Neptune willed differently and we went in
September, the very last day of the season.
There are often two good opportunities, the
first and the last, we at least had a good
one, a perfect day. The few members who
were in town went and lunched together
and had the sweet pleasure of hearing read
a word-bejewelled, poetic phantasy called
“Beautiful Saltair,” written for the club by
our secretary, Mrs. M. A. Lambert.
	On September 30, was rendered an ex-
tremely interesting paper on the sad life
and tragic death of Empress Elizabeth of
Austria. Her life is a most striking illu-
stration that neither wealth nor position can
of itself give happiness.
	Tonight, October 31, 1898, I wish to ex-
press in my farewell to you my heartfelt
thanks for the universal kindness and help-
fulness shown me during the year past by
every member of the club, and I thank you
most cordially for the pleasure and educa-
tion which as president I have been the
happy recipient.
		ROMANIA BUNNELL PRATT.


Mrs. Margaret Black of Glasgow, Scot-
land, is a member of the city board of
education.
	"Current politics affect the lives and
homes of women at various points, from
the cellar furnace to the sons and brothers
in uniform. However closely a woman
may entrench herself in her home and pro-
claim her indifference to politics, the bur-
dens, penalties and results of politics will in
some way find her out. Law and govern-
ment are politics crystallized into power.
The quality of city and state laws and the
character of national policy are determined
by the elections and the men elected. The
mainspring of the whole complicated mech-
anism of American politics and government
is the ballot."

SALT LAKE CITY NOVEMBER 1 & 15, 1898.


OFFICIAL NOTICE.

	At the regular annual business meeting
of the National Woman’s Relief Society
held in the Assembly Hall in this city on
Monday, October 3, at 2 p. m., after con-
siderable discussion of the financial ques-
tion how to provide ways and means for
expenses, Mrs. Zina Young Card suggested
that a day be set apart for a gathering of
the Society and that each member pay on
that particular day ten cents, and mentioned
one or two important dates; it was finally
decided to hold this gathering March 17,
and the following motion was made by
Mrs. Julina L. Smith and seconded by
Mrs. Elizabeth J. Stevenson and others.
Mrs. J. L. Smith’s motion was that the 17th
day of March in each year (commemorating
the day of the first organization of the Soci-
ety in 1842) be set apart for a gathering of
the local Society in each ward in such way
as the respective society decides, and as
seems suitable to its circumstances; that each
member pay into the treasury ten cents on
that day to assist in defraying the neces-
sary expenses of the General Board in the
work of the Society. The motion was put
by the president and carried unanimously
by the members present at the meeting.
	On the following day at the regular
semi-annual meeting of the board of direct-
ors a majority of the members being present
in person, the motion made at the general
meeting on the day previous by Mrs. Julina
L. Smith who offered the motion repeated it
for the approval of the Board. Mrs. Zina
D. H. Young, president of the Board,
stated the motion which had been seconded
by several members and it was carried unan
imously.
	(Signed) ZINA D. H. YOUNG,
						President.
EMMELINE B. WELLS,
				Secretary.


[Column 1]
VISIT TO OMAHA.

NATIONAL COUNCIL OF WOMEN.
	It is known perhaps to most of our read-
ers that some Utah women went to Omaha
to attend the executive sessions of the
National Council of the Women of the
United States. It is not, however, widely
known how important a body the National
Council is consisting of great National
Associations, each of which wields a large

[Column 2]
influence in matters pertaining to the wel-
fare of this great nation.
	Many of our own women who are mem-
bers of the two organizations, the N. W. R.
S. and the Y. L. N. M. I. A., that are
affiliated with the National Council, think it
of little consequence and are hardly suffici-
ently interested to make themselves fami
liar with the standing of the National
Council, but if they could appreciate as
those do who know concerning the matter,
they would rejoice that such opportunities
of enlargement had opened for the women
of these associations, whose work has been
for years given along the lines of uplifting
and awakening thought and activity in all
that tends to the betterment of the condi-
tions of life in the home, in society, in the
state and in the world at large
	“Woman’s hour has struck” there is no
denying that truth. It is everywhere ap-
parent and the time is hastening on when
the women of this inter-mountain region
will be able to prove the great intellectual 
and moral as well as spiritual strength that
has come to them through the forces oper-
ating in their behalf to fulfill the prophecies
that have been made in years gone by with
regard to this chosen land.
	It is the intention of the writer at the
earliest possible opportunity to try to set
forth in an article devoted to the purpose
and merits of the National Council, its bear-
ing upon women, society, reform and all
the great agencies that move the world.
	The Utah women who went to attend the N.
C. W. executive session were Sisters Jane S.
Richards, E. B. Wells, Maria Y. Dougall,
Minnie J. Snow, Susa Young Gates, Joseph-
ine R. West and Margaret A. Caine.
	The first business session opened Monday
morning October 24, at 10 a. m., at which
time after preliminaries, roll call, minutes
and financial statements, questions of im
portance were considered and discussed.
	1.	“Shall the next triennial which is to
be convened in 1899, sit in two houses?”
	2.	“Shall the National Council of Wo-
men of the United States unite in recom-
mending an amendment to the constitution
of the International Council of Women, the
object of which shall be to secure a more
direct representation of the work and inter-
ests of the separate organizations belonging 
to a national council in the International
Council?”
	On these questions those who represented
the two organizations affiliated with the
Council had the opportunity to speak. Mrs.
Jane S. Richards, proxie for the president
of the National Woman’s Relief Society,
and Mrs. E. B. Wells, the delegate, also
Mrs. Maria Y. Dougall, proxie for the
president of the Y. L. N. M. I. A., and
Mrs. Minnie J. Snow, the delegate
	At 3 p. m. Monday afternoon was the
formal opening of the Council.
	Prayer by the Rev. Gerard Andrews and
an address of welcome by Mrs Sawyer,
president of the Woman’s Department of
the Trans-Mississippi International Exposi-
tion; which was one of the best and most
classical addresses we have ever heard, and
the reading of it was like perfect music.
Response by Mrs. May Wright Sewall,
president of the National Council, who
sounded the keynote of the depths of the
Council idea in such a lofty and practical
manner as to awaken every thoughtful wo-
man to activity and call for the unlimited
reserve force in carrying forward the pur-
poses of this magnificent organization. In-

[Column 3]
troduction of presidents and delegates in-
cluding National organizations and Local
councils belonging to the National Coun-
cil, with five minutes response. Mrs. Jane
S. Richards presented in a very sweet,
modest and pleasant manner a brief greet-
ing from the N. W. R. S., and told of some of
the work of the Society. Mrs. Maria Y.
Dougall in a happy and characteristic man-
ner spoke for the young ladies in a few
choice words, rhythmically arranged and
clearly delivered. Utah may well be proud
of her women, both in appearance and the
manner of presenting subjects before an
audience.
	A reception was given the same evening
by the Woman’s Department of the Trans-
Mississippi and International Exposition
which most of the Utah people attended,
all were invited. We noticed Mr. and Mrs.
Dougall and daughter, Mrs. Minnie J. Snow,
Mrs. Susa Young Gates and others. It
was held in the parlors of the Church and
was a very brilliant social affair. The dec-
orations were pink and white roses and
chrysanthemums, refreshments dainty ices
and cakes. Everything was in elegant
style and excellent taste, there was no
over-dressing yet many brilliant costumes 
were worn and jewels flashed here and there
conspicuously.
	Tuesday morning at 9.30 executive ses-
sion, discussion after business on the resolu-
tion considering the care of dependent and
defective children, and whether there should
be a standing committee for that purpose,
also a resolution opposing the plan pursued
by many institutions that breaking up
families by removing the child from the
home and putting additional burdens upon
the community is not the best method, but
if not possible on account of environment
etc. to keep mother and child together, place
children in families instead of public insti-
tutions.
	These resolutions brought out many
thoughts and several able women spoke
with much tender feeling, and it seemed to
be the opinion of the majority that the
question be considered as settled that there
be a standing committee on this subject.
	The afternoon was taken by the lunch-
eon given by the Local Jewish Council, an
account of which will be found in another
column of this issue.
	The evening session was given to frater-
nal delegates with five minutes response
from each and reports of ten minutes from
organizations affiliated with the Council,
after which there were addresses of thirty
minutes on Total Abstinence, Purity and
Woman Suffrage and Society’s Duty to the
Scarlet Woman. This subject was dealt with
by Mrs. Kate Waller Barrett, whom our
readers will remember paid us a visit last
year, and who is one of the most magnetic
and fascinating women who has ever graced
a public platform, and is intensely in
earnest upon this subject. Her speech from
first to last was a lofty and sublime appeal
to all women to lift the fallen whenever
and wherever possible and not forget the
blessed Savior’s example when He said,
“Go and sin no more,” and to insist upon
one standard of morality for both sexes.
	Wednesday, October 29, 9.30 a. m., was
occupied by ten minutes reports from or-
ganizations in the Council. The first given
was by Mrs. Minnie J. Snow, delegate of
the Y. L. N. M. I. A., who presented the work
on the highest intellectual and spiritual
plane, and gave to the theme her own lofty


and exalted word picture, bringing it in full
view to the audience. At 8 p. m. the same
evening among other reports given was the
N. W. R. S., by Mrs. E. B. Wells, and dur-
ing the reading the dear old flag with its
stars and stripes was brought in and the
audience rose en masse and sang America,
which was a very pretty incident.
	On Friday morning at 10 a. m. another
Utah woman, Mrs. Susa Young Gates,
read a paper “The Relation of Capital and
Labor as Applied to Domestic Service.”
The writer believes she may safely say this
paper attracted quite as much attention as
any other and was perhaps an unexpected
surprise to the women on the platform. As
a matter of course we women were all
very proud of Mrs. Gates brilliant effort, in
fact it was very apparent the Utah women
were not behind the times.
	There were many high-bred, intellectual
women from different parts of the country
new to the Council whose personality one
recalls with delight, among these we must
mention Miss Carty of Canada whose beau-
tiful presence bespoke the cultivation of
centuries, and Mrs. Richardson of Boston
whose address upon Citizenship and Loyal-
ty must be ranked as one of the very best
of the many magnificent speeches of the
week in the Council of which we shall treat
in another article more fully.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


EDITORIAL NOTES.

	We are just in receipt of a postal card
from Jerusalem a souvenir of the visit of
the Emperor and Empress of Germany, on
which Madame Mountford writes among
other items, “Greetings to all friends in
Utah.”
	As Christmas is approaching it is well to
remind the public that we have for sale at
this office the book of poems “Musings and
Memories” by E. B. Wells, which is a
suitable holiday gift-book and should be in
every library of the Y. L. M. I. A., cloth
cover $1.25, dainty binding $1.50, moroco
$2.00. This book is handsomely bound and
contains a steel engraving of the author.
	A new book for the holidays will soon be
ready entitled “Aunt Em’s Stories” includ-
ing “Hephzibah,” which was enthusiastic-
ally received when published as a serial ten
years ago, also “Aunt Esther’s Sweetheart,”
“Some Old Love Letters,” “Emily Hamil-
ton’s Christmas,” “He Cometh Not,” and
several others equally as interesting and all
founded on facts.
	President Franklin D. Richards and his
wife, President Jane S. Richards, organized
a Relief Society in Omaha on Tuesday,
October 25, at the residence of one of the
local Elders. There were present also the
presiding Elder of the mission, Elder R.E.
Yeates and Elder Hatch, both of Logan,
Utah, also Bishop Barton and wife of Kays-
ville, and Mrs. Josephine R. West, one of
the General Board of the Primary Associa-
tion, and Mrs. E. B Wells who arrived just
before the meeting closed. Full particulars
will be published.


[Column 1]
GENERAL CONFERENCE.

NATIONAL WOMAN’S RELIEF SOCIETY.
	The October conference of the General
Conference of the National Woman’s Re-
lief Society was held in the Assembly Hall
it in this city, October 5, 1898, commencing

[Column 2]
at 10 a. m., President Zina D. H. Young
presiding. Meeting opened with singing
“Now let us rejoice.” Prayer by President
Jane S. Richards Singing “How firm a
foundation. Roll called, nineteen Stakes
responded. Minutes of the morning meet-
ing of the April conference read and ap-
proved.
	President Zina D. H. Young spoke a few
words of cheer and comfort to the sisters
and bore a strong testimony to the truth of
the Latter-day work, and she said we had
but just commenced our work in this soci-
ety and spoke of the time when the wo-
men of the Church were first organized and
the progress that bad been made, yet felt
that we had only made a start. She spoke
further upon humility and overcoming by
the power of the testimony of the truth un-
til when we shall be able to sing the song
of the redeemed.
	President Jane S. Richards urged the
sisters to be faithful in attending their
meetings and conferences, thought it both
a duty and a pleasure, referred to the genu-
ne help the society had been to the breth-
ren in their respective wards, felt the sis-
ters did step right out as though they were
Elders in Israel, dwelt most earnestly upon
the grain question, said we needed more
granaries and as we had assisted in so many
places to help build ward houses we ought
to have help to build granaries, quoted
Bishop Hunter on the grain question and
knew he was prompted by the spirit of the
Lord when he urged the sisters to take care
of the wheat, there is no danger of having
too much and the time is sure to come
when it will be needed even if we do have
bounteous harvests. Advised the sisters to
hold their meetings at the time appointed,
the angels might be there waiting. Sister
Richards also spoke upon kindness and for-
giving each other, let us try and bear our
trials and be thankful we have a place with
His people.
	Sister Zina said: “They that, feared the
Lord spoke often one to another and the
Lord hearkened and heard and said they
shall be mine when I make up my jewels.”
Counselor B. W. Smith referred to the
bounteous harvest and the great need of
laying up grain she urged the sisters to
look well after the sick, afflicted and dying
—when they pass away see that everything
is done properly, and teach purity, truthful-
ness, virtue and industry, don’t go outside
of your income, don’t go into debt. It
will not be long before we who are advanc-
ing in years will meet our loved ones and
enter into joy. “I have great joy in my
labors in the Temple, and sisters prepare
yourselves to work for your dead, may
you be inspired to do this.”
	President F. A Hammond represented
San Juan in the absence of his wife, the
president. The wheat question he thought
seemed to be the text, he thought it was
the proper subject for the time—bread is
the staff of life, the sisters in our Stake are
doing well with the wheat and when it is
not available are storing flour; this year the
earth smiles and responds beautifully to the
husbandsman, out of one crop of dry farm-
ing which seeded itself largely himself and
sons had raised 1900 bushels. The speaker
also alluded to the franchise and urged wo-
men to vote for good men and women who
will stand for the home, for virtue and
holiness, praised the sisters of that Stake for
their diligence and good works and in going
to their conferences when sometimes they

[Column 3]
had to travel fifty miles some of them to at-
tend. President Hammond spoke for the
“Woman’s Exponent,” said it ought to be
better sustained, felt every president and
every teacher in the society should take it
and read it, he enjoyed reading it and al-
ways had, knew people plead not being able
but he did not believe there was a woman
there who could not afford to take it, only
one dollar a year, this, he continued, is the
grandest and strongest organization we
have in these valleys, and let us sustain it
as we should.
	Brother John Beck thanked Sister Zina,
the president, for inviting him to speak.
in his remarks, after encouraging the sis-
ters in their good work, he said that it
seemed to him that the time had come when
the daughters of Eve were so that they were
doing as much as the sons of Adam, and he
thought that the marriage question was one
that women should settle. Made some
suggestions about the grain and the build-
ing of elevators in every Stake of Zion.
	Sister Orms spoke for Pocatello Stake
and the newness of the organization, only a
little over two months, knew they had a
good people, there were faithful sisters in
the society.
	Singing, Doxology. Benediction by
President Hammond.
				E. B. WELLS, Sec.


A TRIBUTE TO AUNT MELISSA.

	Dear Aunt Melissa was the eldest daughter
of Cornelius P. and Permelia Darrow Lott.
Our grandparents were born in the state of
New York and were both of the old Rev-
olutionary ancestors who fought in battle
and held positions of trust in the Govern-
ment during its seven years struggle for
American independence. They were the
only ones of either family to join the
Church. Grandpa came of a fine old Dutch
family, and grandma of the early Puritan
stock. Grandpa moved to Pennsylvania
prior to his marriage, as did also the Dar-
row family. Aunt Melissa was born in
that state in Luzern Co., on the 9th of
February, 1823.
	People who knew her say she was a
beautiful, refined, intelligent girl; those
who knew her best loved her most. At
the age of nineteen years she was married
to the Prophet Joseph Smith and lived with
his wife Emma (whom she dearly loved) in
the “Nauvoo Mansion” until after the
prophet’s martyrdom. She then returned
to her father’s home, shared all the hard-
ships of the Latter-day Saints in their vari-
ed travels from state to state, suffering-
p[-]ivations of every name and nature un-
complainingly. Being the eldest of a fam-
ly of eleven children and her mother being
of a delicate organization her duties were
necessarily great, her patience often tried
and her physical cares many, yet she was
ever gentle, kind and cheerful under the
most trying circumstances. She came to
Utah with her parents in 1848 and married
Ira Willis with a perfect understanding that
she would be the prophet's wife in the
Great Beyond.
	Aunt Melissa had born to her seven chil-
dren, four sons and three daughters. Her
husband, two sons and two daughters pre-
ceded her to the other side. She left two
sons, one daughter, a host of grandchildren,
one sister, two brothers and many loved
relatives and friends who will miss her all
the time, for she was an exceptional wo-


man; one of great strength of character,
bright perception, pure, just, generous
principles. Her advice and example were
always for good, her every act displayed
the unselfish, noble womanhood; we all re-
garded her as one of nature’s grandest type
of heroines. From earliest infancy her life
has been one of sacrifice, privation, mental
and physical suffering, yet when others
complain we have heard her say many
times, “No I do not wish my life-trials
other than they are, ’tis best for God de-
signed it so or it would not be.” The
prophet promised her that in taking the
step with him in her girlhood “she should
be the savior of her father’s household.”
We all expect and have faith that this will
be fulfilled.
	Aunt was a true, consistent Latter-day
Saint, her last years were spent in comfort-
ing the aged and lonely and ministering to
the sick and afflicted. She was up to with-
in a few months of her death an active,
earnest worker in the Relief Society at Lehi,
Utah Co., and we believe one of the presid-
ing officers. She had done a great deal of
Temple work, made Temple suits, laid out
the dead and aided in every possible way
God’s children.
	To her immediate family she was a just,
loving, loyal, capable mother, sister and
friend, a peacemaker in very deed. Filled
with sterling integrity she died firm in the
faith of a glorious resurrection. She was
buried on the 15th of July, (in her seventy-
fifth year) in the Salt Lake cemetery on
the lot where her parents, husband and
children are sleeping. 
	God grant we may follow in the path she
has left behind, keep firm in the faith of
Christ’s Gospel, thereby prove worthy to
meet her on the other shore when our time
shall come to go.
			Affectionately
				J. P. M. FARNSWORTH,
					139 Brigham Street.
	Oct. 28, 1898.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


U. W. P. C. 

	The annual meeting of the U. W. P. C.
was held at the residence of L. B. Bunnell,
Oct. 31, 1898, President R. B Pratt in the
chair. Opening prayer was offered by Mary
A. Freeze. Roll call was answered by
sentiments from Lydia D. Alder’s poems,
followed by instrumental music by the
hostess. Unfinished business was called
for and that of incorporation was again
postponed. The president said that a
business meeting had been called in regu-
lar order and the officers for the ensuing
year nominated. The question of balloting
or voting in the new members was dis-
cussed at some length and finally Dr. Shipp
made a motion that we elect our new
officers by vote; this motion carried unani-
mously and the president then presented
the following names which were elected
without a dissenting voice:
	Ella W. Hyde, president; Isabella
Cameron Brown, 1st vice-president; Caro-
line C. R. Wells, 2nd vice-president;
Aimed Schiller, secretary; Ruth M. Fox,
assistant secretary; Lydia D. Alder, cor-
responding secretary; Edith R. Lovsey,
assistant corresponding secretary; Margaret
A. Caine, treasurer; Ellis R. Shipp, histor-
ian; Helen V. Kimball, Dr. Ellis. R. Shipp,
Dr. Romania B. Pratt program committee.
	Miss Raleigh then sang in a very rich, full


voice "The Miller’s Daughter." The
president then made a very eloquent fare-
well speech, giving an interesting word
picture and resume of the year’s work, after
which the following program was rendered:
Miss Kimball gave a most interesting ac-
count of the late Queen of Denmark and her
beautiful daughters. Mrs. Alder also gave
some pleasing reminiscences of the king’s
court and the Princess Alexandria who
created a revolution among the nobility re-
garding children. She took her babies
everywhere and when she attended grand
dinners, "entertained the company with
what the baby said." Miss Kimball opened
the discussion on the Dreyfus case and read
some newspaper clippings. The treasurer,
Mrs. Harker, then read the report for the
past year which was very satisfactory.
This was followed by a very touching song
in the very sweet voice of Mrs. Maggie Bas-
sett. Then with the brilliant lights extin-
guished and in the soft glow of the grate
fires Mrs. Ruth M. Fox related some very
thrilling Hallowe’en stories, greatly to the
amusement of her listeners. Mrs. Mamie
Stringham favored the company with classic-
al music and the refreshments that followed
were of the same order of excellence.
	A vote of thanks was [-]endered the hostess
for her kindly entertainment and to the
president for her very efficient service to
the club during the year, and the new
officers were duly installed.
ELLIS R. SHIPP, Act. Sec.


[Column 1]
LADIES SEMI-MONTHLY MEETING.

	Minutes of the Ladies Semi-monthly
meeting held in the 14th ward Assembly
hall, on Saturday, September 17, 1898,
Counselor B. W. Smith presiding in the
absence of President Horne. Singing “O,
say what is truth.” Prayer by Sister
Julia C. Howe. Singing “We thank Thee
O, God for a prophet.”
	Sister Smith said that Sister Eliza R.
Snow had said that there would be a soci-
ety formed that would be as the school of
prophets to the sisters. Observed that she
was not as punctual as she was in her
younger days, but she always enjoyed the
meetings and the testimonies of the sisters.
We should make good resolutions when we
meet together and carry them out in our
lives.
	Sister Annie T. Hyde said these meet-
ings had done her a great deal of good, she
had tried hard to overcome her timidity and
had found this was a good school for wo-
men. Spoke of the many changes that had
come to us, and of the death of our beloved
President Woodruff. She had been well
acquainted with him, could rememember
when she was a child of his honest and
honorable character. Thought we should
all have the love of Christ in our hearts and
not criticise or backbite each other, and
that the example that parents should set for
their children should be exemplary. We
mothers should know where our children are.
	Sister Howe said she would like to speak
so as to be heard if possible, and would say
what the Lord dictated. She always taught
the children when she presided in the Pri-
mary not to steal or be disobedient, she felt
anxious for the children of Zion. The
Prophet Joseph had said the Relief Society
should assist in correcting the morals, we
should exercise faith and grow stronger.
Alluded to President Woodruff, what an
exemplary man he was in all things, men-

[Column 2]
tioned a sermon of John W. Taylor’s in
which he said that spiritual gifts did and
must exist in the Church, and we should
live to exercise these gifts. Let us not be
guilty of having any gifts from Satan, let
no contention exist among us but be filled
with charity and love.
	Sisters Rawlings and Brown and others
bore testimony to the goodness of God and
said many excellent things, rejoiced in
spiritual gifts and the work in the Temple,
felt thankful for such blessed opportunities.
	Sister Margaret P. Young said she
thought the sisters who had expressed
themselves were very useful, for their
words were to the effect that they were in-
terested in each other and striving to do
each other good. Thought we should pray
for and appreciate those sisters who presided
over us as they were growing aged in body,
but were still filled with ambition for the
work of God. Felt that the golden mo-
ments that God grants to us should be
spent in getting precious knowledge.
	Sister Jane M. E. Young was called up-
on by Counselor B. W. Smith and gave
some of her experiences in visiting the
State Penitentiary, she thought we should
correct all evil and profanity we hear among
children, more especially boys are inclined
to this evil. Spoke of President Woodruff
and the counsel he had given her, what a
comfort his words had been. Alluded to
his economy of time. “Dear sisters, I do
pray that we will be able to train and
rear our children in a pleasing manner unto
God; this is my humble prayer.”
	Sister Hyde said she wished to bear her
testimony of the goodness of God, said He
was good and that His eye was over us.
	Other sisters also bore testimony and ex-
pressed many good wishes. Counselor
Smith said she hoped we would all meet
again in two weeks and bring our neigh-
bors with us.
	Meeting adjourned for two weeks. Sing-
ing “The time is far spent.” Benediction
by Sister Annie T. Hyde.
JULIA S. WOOLLEY, Sec. pro tem.

	Minutes of Ladies Semi-monthly meeting
held in the 14th ward Assembly hall,
Saturday, October 1, 1898, Counselor E. J.
Stevenson presiding. Opened by singing
“Our God we raise to Thee.” Prayer by
Counselor Webb. Singing “O my Father.”
Minutes of meetings held August 28 and
September 17 were read and approved.
	Sister Stevenson felt to thank God for
the rain, also for the opportunity of meet-
ing with the sisters. Realized that the
spirit of God was present and if we all en-
couraged that spirit we could have a grand
time, for the Lord has said “Where two or
three shall meet together in my name, I
will be in their midst and that to bless
them.” Spoke briefly on the death of our
beloved President Woodruff, said how meek
and humble he always was and exhorted
the sisters to follow his example. Did not
know the reason President Horne was ab-
sent, perhaps it was due to the inclemency
of the weather, hoped she was not sick.
Gave the meeting into the hands of the
sisters.
	Sister Jane James praised the Lord that
she had seen the Prophet Joseph Smith and
that he had blest her. Spoke also of the
kindness shown to her by Sister Emma
Smith, wife of the Prophet Joseph Smith.
Desired to be humble as a little child, that
she might be worthy to enter one of the
mansions that the Lord had prepared.


Sister Webb said we were living in
momentous times, exhorted the sisters to
live so that we might avoid the sins and
evils that surround us and prayed that the
young people might be preserved from the
snares that beset them. Testified that the
Relief Society had been a great blessing to
her.
	Sister Parkes of Idaho, though a com-
parative stranger in our midst could testify
to the truth of the work. She was well
acquainted with the Prophet Joseph Smith.
Remembered the time the Relief Society
was organized, heard the prophet say if
the sisters would be faithful that the angels
would visit them. Told them that the
Lord was no respector of persons, but if
they would always live near to Him He
would grant their desires. She had proven
his words true and prayed that she might
always be among the faithful workers of the
Relief Society.
	Sister Longmore rejoiced in the work and
hoped to prove faithful to the end.
	Sister Rossiter said the Lord had be-
stowed upon her many blessings and she
desired to spend her life in His service.
Spoke of the time when she received the
Gospel, desired to do all she could for her
dead relatives.
	Sister Julia Golightly was trying to
overcome her weaknesses that she might
gain the approval of her Heavenly Father.
	Sister Mary P. Silver spoke of the joy
she felt in the everlasting Gospel, she had
left all for it and the Lord had blest her
abundantly.
	Sister Eliza Rossiter was thankful for
the Gospel, knew it was true and hoped to
prove faithful to the end.
	Sister Alder said she had received many
blessings from her Heavenly Father, had
also many trials, yet she tried not to com-
plain. She often wondered what we should
do without the Gospel, we should surely
faint by the wayside. “Sisters let us serve
the Lord through evil and through good
report and ever hold on to the iron rod.”
Encouraged the sisters to trust in the Lord
and not think too much of their sorrows,
to remember the Lord is on our side all the
time.
	Meeting adjourned for two weeks. Sing-
ing “The God we adore.” Benediction by
Hannah M. Wright.
		HANNAH M. WRIGHT, Act. Sec.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


IN MEMORIAM.

MARIA CROMPTON.
	Sister Maria Crompton, wife of Robert Cromp
ton died at her home in American Fork on
Thursday, October 13, 1898, after a long and pain-
ful illness of nearly two years, the cause being
dropsy superinduced by heart trouble. She was
born April 8, 1834, at Withyham, Sussex, Eng-
land, joined the Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-
day Saints in New York City and came to Utah in
1861. She was married to Brother Crompton in
1862 and has made her home in American Fork
ever since. She was the mother of seven chil-
dren six of whom survive her, three sons and
three daughters, also a number of grandchildren.
	Sister Crompton was a teacher in the Relief
Society for a number of years, always w[-]lling to
do her part in the reat work that devolves
upon the sisters in this calling, was faithful in at-
ending her meetings as long as she was able and
rejoiced in the testimony of Jesus. She was pa-
tient and long suffering during her illness and had
unbounded faith in the ordi[-]ance of the Church
for the healing of the sick, always obtaining re-
lief whenever she was administered to by the
Elders. She was a devoted wife and mother, al-
ways working diligently to set a good example
for her children and to educate them in the prin-


ciples of life and salvation as well as to fit them to
battle with the trials of life. Great credit is due
to her children who worked so untiringly in her
behalf during her illness, never wearying in their
labor of love. May the Lord support them and
their father upon whom the loss falls so heavily,
and may He help them to realize that all is well
and though her presence will be sadly missed in
the home circle, yet she may be a link in the
chain that binds them to the Eternal Father.
	EMMA FEATHERSTONE,
	JANE C. HINDLEY,
	SYLVIA MISENER,
				Committee.


[Column 1]	
THE NATIONAL COUNCIL OF
WOMEN.

PRESIDENT SEWALL’S ADDRESS.
	The stirring events of the last six months
have been so emphasized on public opinion
that they seem to have occupied the entire
year. The din of war is wont so to deafen
the public ear that the quiet activities of a
war period are to it inaudible. There can
have been no time when war did not involve
the women of countries engaged in it; for
even before women had any participation in
public affairs the wars that drew men from
their homes increased the importance of wo-
men in private affairs as their industrial
activity was augmented perforce by the very
events which made the greatest drafts upon
their hearts. At no period of stress in our
country’s history have the public services
of American women in various fields been
so conspicuous as during the last six
months. The services of Miss Helen
Gould in pecuniary gifts, or Clara Barton
with the sign of the Red Cross, of the hun-
dreds of nurses who have shunned no
danger of climate and no discomfort of
camp, of the women war correspondents
who, let it be hoped, have contributed no
yellow pages to the journals of the day,
only conspicuously illustrate the various
ways in which hundreds of thousands of
women have, on a smaller scale, contributed
to their country’s strength.
	While it was still hoped that war might
be averted the National Council responding
to the well known desire of President Mc-
Kinley, added to the numerous petitions
that went out from brave and loyal
men and women from all over the country,
its petition representing the desire of over
1,000,000 women, to avoid war if possible.
That no one desired to avert the catastrophe
more than President McKinley his whole
course of conduct showed; that he was
grateful to those who attempted to hold the
public mind calm and to afford time for dip-
lomatic intervention, his response to such
petitions amply proves. It may well be
believed that from the day war was declared
those who have tried in whatever circle
they were, to avert it were most fervently
grateful for the magnanimity constantly
shown by our chief executive, the human-
ity manifested by our generals and the hero-
ism demonstrated on land and sea by our
countrymen.
THREE NEW COMMITTEES.
	Three most important standing commit-
tees have been added during the last year to
our groups of committee workers. The
committee on social peace and international
arbitration, with the Countess Cora Slo-
comb di Brazzi at its head. The sudden
call to arms but served to emphasize the
need of the committee and to show that
such a committee should long ago have
been organized and been active in the diffu-

[Column 2]
sion of its doctrines. The committee on
social purity, with Rev. Anna Garlin
Spencer at its head. The formation of
this committee is a declaration that the
National Council of Women of the United
States is committed to that doctrine of the
common moral standard for men and wo-
men so forcibly expressed in the well known
phrase, “A white life for two.” The
committee on domestic relations under the
law, whose chairman is Miss Octavia Wil-
liams Bates of Detroit. It has for its
specific work such a codification of the laws
bearing upon domestic life as will show the
contradiction, existing among our states and
the manner in which these contradictions
work injustice to individuals and evil to
the community.
	During the year but one national organ-
zation of women has entered the council
ranks—the Rathbone Sisters—the second of
the great orders that have come in, the
Ladies of the Maccabees being the other
one.
	The growth through the multiplication
of local councils is not less important. The
largest formed during the year is that at
New Orleans, which already includes in its
membership 114 local organizations. Suc-
cessful initiative has been taken in organiz-
ing a local council in New York City.
	Among the organizations that for the first
time send fraternal delegates to our sessions
are the Congress of Mothers and the George
Washington Memorial Association.
	During the last year the fraternal rela-
tions with Canada, which have from the
beginning of council work been fostered in
the two countries, have been emphasized.
WOMEN AND WAR.
	In these days we are continually hear-
ing of the new role which the United States
is to play among the nations of the earth
We are told that it will no longer do for
the United States to hold itself in isolation
from the rest of the world: that whether
it wishes it or not the nation is destined to
assume a place which will compel all other-
nations to reckon with it in the prosecu-
tion of any projects they may devise. Those
of us who truly believe that a perfect social
peace would make either a civil or a foreign
war impossible must still rejoice in every
good that seems to have resulted from the
recent war. If by it our country shall
be lifted out of a selfish materialism which
has engrossed it into a sense of its perman-
ent relationship with all the other nations
of the earth this must be counted a great
good. But we believe it is its happy des-
tiny to prove its relationship to the nations
of the world and its consciousness of its
kinship, not by becoming a terror because
of its invincible navy, invulnerable ships
and unmatched soldiery, but rather be-
cause of its sense of human equality, which
is the inevitable outcome of a true sense of
human kinship.
	Our country has been isolated by its
size and by the satisfaction which it derived
from contemplating its own dimensions.
Its sense of international relationship will
be quickened by a sense of the insignificance
of size when disconnected from contents
and quality. Of this new sense of kinship
among the nations, which indisputably
has been quickened into consciousness by
the recent war, the council was a forerun-
ner. Through the International Council of
Women this sense of relationship among
the nations has already assumed organic


form, and, as is well known, organic form
is essential to conscious and responsible
activity.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]
WOMEN MEET AT LUNCHEON.

BRILLIANT COMPANY.
	The luncheon and reception tendered by
the local Council of Jewish Women to the
visiting delegates of the National Coun-
cil of Women at the Metropolitan club on
Oct. 25, was a most brilliant entertainment.
It is seldom that one cit[-] has the pleasure
of entertaining so many distinguished wo-
men as are sojourning in the Gate City.
The spacious hall which was well adapted
for the purpose of entertaining on a grand
scale, was magnificent with myriads of
electric lights and flowers, and exquisite
confections in costuming, scintillating with
gems, vied with natural fiowers in the
adorning of these women, who although
they dive deep into reforms advanced to
elevate humanity and raise the standard of
citizenship, do not despise the witchery of
becoming apparel the glitter of jewels and
perfume of flowers.
	For a short time the receiving matrons,
Mrs Belle A. Polack, Mrs. Simon Adamsky,
and Mrs. Moritz Meyer, stood in the hall.
Only there was nothing about it to suggest
a hall, as it had been em[-]owered with ex-
otics so that it might easily have been im-
agined a bower in some tropical isle.
Passing thence to the long dining room the
guests perceived that the floral decorations
had been managed with artistic restraint.
The tables were arranged in a geometrical
design and beautifully decorated with a rich
display of white and yellow chrysanthe-
mums. At the head of the banquet board
the five guests of honor sat. Mrs. Polack,
president of the local Council of Jewish
Women, in the center. On her right was
seated Mrs. Hannah G. Solomon of Chica-
go, on her left Mrs. May Wright Sewall of
Indianapolis. Miss Susan B. Anthony sat
next to Mrs. Solomon and Mrs. Draper
Smith was seated next to Mrs. Sewall.
	The out of-town guests who occupied
spaces about the board were: Susan B. An-
thony, Mrs. Hannah Solomon of Chicago,
Mrs. Simon of Kansas City, Mo., Mrs.
Rosenwasser of Cleveland, Mrs. Iesner of
Champaign, Ill., Mrs. Foster, Mrs. Kate
Barrett of Washington, D. C., Mrs. Emme-
line B. Wells, Mrs. M. Y. Dougall of Salt
Lake City, Mrs. Henry Hirsch of Des
Moines. Mrs Charles Mayer, Miss Mayer
of Lincoln, Mrs. Oberfelder of Sidney, Mrs.
Hirshland of Des Moines, Mrs. Lipals, Mrs.
Simon, Mrs. Ryder of Kansas City, Mrs.
Sewall of Indianapolis, Mrs Barbe of Chi-
cago, Mrs. Thershein, Mrs. Schulman, Mrs.
Fuhrman of Seattle, Mrs. Hollister of De-
troit, Mrs. Richards of Champaign, Mrs.
Moog, Mrs. Anna Shaw of Philadelphia, Mrs.
Frances Beauchamp of Lexington, Ky., Mrs.
Louise B. Robbins of Adrian, Mich., Mrs. L.
S. Lieberman, Mrs. Simon of Kansas City,
Miss Octave Williams Bates of Detroit, Mrs.
Newberry Adams of Dubuque.
GREETINGS TO THE GUESTS.
	In opening the post-prandial exercises
Mrs. Clara Rosewater, toastmistress, greet-
ed the guests, saying: "At no time in the
history of Omaha have so many brilliant
and talented women been with us. I there-
fore think it fitting that the session of the
Council of Jewish Women close with an ex-
pression of goodwill and fraternal feeling."

[Column 2]
She called the first toast of the afternoon,
"Welcome."
	Mrs. Belle Polack responded to this toast
in a happy manner, extending the most
cordial welcome to all.
	Mrs. Rosewater then said: "During the
last few years woman has steadily moved
on. She has advanced from the seclusion
of the past. The club is largely responsi-
ble for this."
	"What Clubbing has Done for Women"
was responded to by Mrs. H. F. Smith,
president of the Woman's Club of Omaha.
She spoke slowly and distinctly. "Club-
bing punishes," she said, “punishes for
past offences. We see our shortcomings in
the light of present study and regret times
wasted in youth. Clubbing injures physic-
ally unless taken in homeopathic doses.
To be serious it brings into our midst for
our enjoyment here today the leaders in the
world’s progress and thought.”
	“The Wonder of the Nineteenth Century:
the Fairest Work o[-] the Great Author”
was responded to by Mrs. May Wright
Sewall, and “Liberalism in Religion” by
Rev. Mary Gerard Andrews. "If we grow
intellectually we must grow into the
thought of the spirit,” said Mrs. Andrews,
“in the spirit of love. In spite of our dif-
ferences there is a large spirit of love and
good fellowship and this is the spirit of
liberal religion in our club.”
	Miss Susan B. Anthony was presented to
respond to the toast “Woman is man’s
Equal in Intellect, in Character His Su-
perior. She Needs no Eulogy. She
Speaks for Herself.” Miss Anthony was
heartily greeted as she arose to speak and
said: “The other half of genus homo is
making its appearance in the world. For
the last quarter of a century I have been
trying to impress upon the other half that
she has the inherent right to be equal to
the original half at the ballot box. I hope
to see the fruition of my labors.”
	Mrs. Leo Franklin responded to “Cas-
tles in the Air,” and Mrs. Hannah Solo-
mon “Why Omaha Needs a Jewish
Council.”

THE

	Denver is one day nearer Chicago to-day than
it was yesterday. This is the actual practical
effect of the fast mail trains inaugurated by the
North-Western and Union Pacific.—Chicago
Times Herald, Feb. 8, 1898.
"The Chicago Special"
Leaves Denver, 4:30 p m today.
Arrives Omaha, 7:00 a m tomorrow.
Arrives Chicago, 8:45 p m tomorrow.
	To the Chicago, Union Pacific and North-
western belongs the credit of inaugurating the
new fast train service between Denver and
Chicago.
	This new train is a triumph of modern rail-
roading. It consists of six magnificent cars:
Buffet, smoking and library car, with barber
shop, etc. Two splendid palace sleeping cars.
Reclining chair car. Dining car, meals a la
carte. Baggage car.
	This is the only train between Denver and
Chicago having the luxurious
........Buffet, Smoking and Library Cars.
	Westbound, "The Colorado Special" leaves
Chicago 10:00 a. m. arrives Omaha 11:55 p m, to-
day, and Denver 1:30 p m, tomorrow.
